title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
paddling pool /ˈpadlɪŋ ˌpuːl/ · noun
A shallow body of water in which one can comfortably wade rather than swim — a place to get the feel of the water before going further.
This site is the statistical equivalent: a low-pressure refresher in the analyses you’ll need for your thesis or specialist master’s courses. Work through topics at your own pace, watch short JASP demonstrations, and practise with real datasets — a shallow place to revisit the essentials before going further with your own research.

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
How to use this site

Start with Getting Started if you have not used JASP before, or need a quick refresh on loading data and navigating the interface.
Work through Basics — descriptive statistics, t-tests, ANOVA, regression, non-parametric tests, and contingency tables. These are the foundations that almost every analysis builds on.
Consult Advanced Topics when your thesis or course requires a specific technique. Not everyone needs all of these — pick what is relevant to you.

Each topic page follows the same structure:






NoteWhat you will find on each topic page




What is it? — a short conceptual overview
When to use it — decision guide: which research question, which data type
Assumptions — what to check before interpreting your results
How to do it in JASP — menu paths and links to short screen recordings in the JASP Video Library
Interpreting the output — what to report and how to read the numbers
Further reading — textbook chapters (DSUJ, Agresti, plus discipline-specific references on the advanced pages) and blog posts
Further viewing — links to the full SSR lecture recording for the topic, where applicable
Try it yourself — concrete exercises with a linked JASP dataset and a worked solution

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
Basics
These topics cover the analyses expected of every master’s student.


Descriptive Statistics
Summarise and visualise your data before running any inferential test. Mean, SD, histograms, boxplots, raincloud plots.


t-Tests
Compare one or two means. One-sample, paired-samples, and independent-samples t-tests — with effect sizes and assumption checks.


ANOVA
Compare three or more groups, or multiple factors. One-way, factorial, ANCOVA, and repeated-measures ANOVA.


Correlation & Regression
Quantify relationships and predict outcomes. Pearson correlation, simple regression, and multiple regression.


Non-Parametric Tests
Rank-based alternatives for ordinal data, small samples, strong outliers, or non-normal distributions. Mann–Whitney (independent t-test), Wilcoxon signed-rank (paired t-test), Kruskal–Wallis (one-way ANOVA), and Friedman (repeated-measures ANOVA).


Contingency Tables
Test associations between two categorical variables. Chi-square tests of independence, Fisher’s exact, and Cramér’s V.

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
Advanced Topics
These analyses appear frequently in master’s theses. Choose the ones relevant to your research area.


Mediation & Moderation
Test whether an effect works through a third variable (mediation) or depends on a third variable (moderation). Uses the PROCESS module.


Logistic Regression
Predict a binary outcome (yes/no, clinical/control) from one or more predictors. Odds ratios and classification accuracy.


Factor Analysis
Identify underlying dimensions in a set of items. Exploratory (EFA) and Confirmatory (CFA) approaches, plus PCA.


Power Analysis
Determine how many participants you need to detect an effect of a given size. Avoid underpowered studies.


Missing Data & Imputation
Understand the types of missingness (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and when multiple imputation is appropriate.


Meta-Analysis
Synthesise effect sizes across studies. Forest plots, heterogeneity, and moderator analysis in JASP.


Measurement Invariance
Test whether a scale behaves the same way across groups. Configural, metric, scalar, and strict invariance via multi-group CFA.


Path Modeling
Model systems of directed relationships among variables. Recursive vs. nonrecursive models, direct and indirect effects, full SEM.


Latent Class / Profile Analysis
Identify unobserved subgroups of people from categorical (LCA) or continuous (LPA) indicators. A person-centred approach.


Network Analysis
Conceptualise constructs as networks of interacting components rather than reflections of a latent factor. GGM, regularisation, centrality.

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
Reference pages
Two cross-cutting pages that pull together material referenced from every topic.


Which test should I use?
A decision flowchart and lookup table from your research question and data to the matching test, with links into the relevant topic page.


Reporting & effect sizes
APA-style reporting templates for every test on this site, alongside the standard effect-size conventions and the most common reporting mistakes to avoid.

title: Reporting & effect sizes
text:
Reference
The standards for reporting statistical results have converged in the last decade. This page collects the APA-style reporting templates and effect-size conventions that appear scattered across the topic pages, in one place you can use as a write-up checklist.

title: Reporting & effect sizes
text:
Effect-size conventions

WarningDon’t rely on the labels alone



Cohen’s benchmarks were derived from typical effect sizes in 1960s psychology. They overstate what “large” means in many modern domains. Where you can, anchor effect sizes against:

A meta-analytic estimate for similar studies.
A smallest effect size of interest (SESOI) justified on theoretical or practical grounds (see the Power Analysis page for the SESOI argument).
The effect size you would consider substantively meaningful in your specific application.

title: Reporting & effect sizes
text:
APA reporting templates
The templates below use APA 7th-edition conventions. Italicise statistical symbols (t, F, p, r, N); don’t italicise variable names.

t-test (independent samples)

An independent-samples t-test showed that anxiety ratings of participants exposed to a picture of a spider (M = 47.0, SD = 9.3, n = 12) were higher than those of participants exposed to a real spider (M = 40.0, SD = 9.3, n = 12), t(21.4) = 1.68, p = .108, d = 0.71, 95% CI [-0.12, 1.53].

Note: Welch’s df reported as a decimal (21.4) because it adjusts for unequal variances.


t-test (paired samples)

A paired-samples t-test indicated that anxiety scores were lower at follow-up (M = 32.5, SD = 7.1) than at baseline (M = 41.2, SD = 8.0; N = 48 pairs), t(47) = 5.32, p < .001, d = 0.77, 95% CI [0.45, 1.08].



One-way ANOVA

A one-way ANOVA examined the effect of music type on dancing time. There were significant differences between the three groups, F(2, 57) = 4.32, p = .018, η² = .13, 95% CI [.01, .27]. Tukey HSD post-hoc tests showed that the rock group (M = 23.4, SD = 5.1) danced longer than the classical group (M = 16.7, SD = 4.8, p = .021), but did not differ from the silence group (M = 19.2, SD = 6.0, p = .089).



Repeated-measures ANOVA (with sphericity correction)

A repeated-measures ANOVA on serial recall showed a significant effect of music type, F(1.76, 46.5) = 5.21, p = .011, η²_g = .17 (Greenhouse-Geisser corrected because Mauchly’s test indicated a violation of sphericity, p = .034).



Correlation

Hours of sleep and exam performance were positively correlated, r(98) = .42, p < .001, 95% CI [.24, .57].



Simple regression

Album sales were significantly predicted by advertising budget, b = 0.096, 95% CI [0.076, 0.115], β = .578, t(196) = 9.98, p < .001. The model explained 33% of the variance in album sales, F(1, 198) = 99.6, p < .001, adjusted R² = .33.



Multiple regression

A multiple regression with three predictors (advertising, radio airplay, band image) significantly predicted album sales, F(3, 196) = 129.5, p < .001, adjusted R² = .66. Each predictor contributed significantly: advertising, β = .51, t(196) = 12.26, p < .001; radio airplay, β = .51, t(196) = 12.51, p < .001; band image, β = .19, t(196) = 4.59, p < .001.



Hierarchical regression

A hierarchical regression entered demographic controls in Model 0 (R² = .04), added extraversion in Model 1 (ΔR² = .03, ΔF(1, 256) = 8.41, p = .004), and narcissism in Model 2 (ΔR² = .02, ΔF(1, 255) = 6.32, p = .012). Narcissism predicted status-update frequency above and beyond extraversion, β = .15, t(255) = 2.51, p = .012.



Logistic regression

A binary logistic regression predicting recovery from age and treatment group was significant, χ²(2) = 14.3, p < .001, Nagelkerke R² = .23. Treatment group significantly predicted recovery, b = 1.42, OR = 4.14, 95% CI [1.87, 9.17], p = .001 — patients in the treatment condition had over four times the odds of recovery compared with controls.



Chi-square test of independence

A chi-square test of independence showed a significant association between age group and party preference, χ²(4, N = 487) = 18.6, p < .001, Cramér’s V = .24. Inspection of the standardised residuals indicated that voters aged 51+ were over-represented among Party A supporters (z = 2.8).



Mediation

The total effect of age on gossiping was significant (b = 0.14, 95% CI [0.07, 0.20]). The indirect effect through perceived mate value was significant (ab = 0.06, bootstrapped 95% CI [0.02, 0.11]) — supporting partial mediation. The direct effect remained significant (b = 0.08, 95% CI [0.02, 0.14]).



Non-parametric test (Wilcoxon rank-sum / Mann–Whitney)

A Wilcoxon rank-sum test showed that shopping duration differed between men (Mdn = 47, IQR = 33) and women (Mdn = 62, IQR = 41), W = 312, p = .008, rank-biserial r = .42, 95% CI [.13, .65].



Meta-analysis

A random-effects meta-analysis of k = 18 studies (total N = 2,341) yielded a pooled effect of d = 0.43, 95% CI [0.28, 0.58], z = 5.60, p < .001. Heterogeneity was moderate, Q(17) = 34.2, p = .007, I² = 50%. There was no evidence of publication bias based on Egger’s test (t(16) = 1.21, p = .24).



SEM / path model

The hypothesised structural model fitted the data acceptably, χ²(45) = 78.4, p = .002, CFI = .961, RMSEA = .052 [.034, .069], SRMR = .047. The indirect effect of X on Y through M was significant (standardised ab = .14, bootstrapped 95% CI [.07, .23]).

title: Reporting & effect sizes
text:
Things to avoid






WarningCommon reporting mistakes




“p > .05, so there is no effect.” A non-significant result means the data didn’t provide strong evidence for an effect, not that the effect is zero. Report the estimate with its CI and let the reader judge.
Rounding p to “.000”. Always write “< .001” instead — p is never exactly zero.
Reporting only p with no effect size. With large N, every test is “significant”; without an effect size, the reader can’t tell whether the result matters.
Reporting the standardised coefficient (β) without the unstandardised (b). Many readers want the original-units interpretation for translation back to practice.
Italicising the wrong things. Variable names (age, condition) are not italicised; statistical symbols (t, F, p, r, N, M, SD) are. df is italicised; brackets are not.
Mixing decimals. Use leading zeros for statistics that can exceed 1 (M = 0.42); drop leading zeros for statistics bounded by 1 (p = .42, r = .42, β = .42).

title: Reporting & effect sizes
text:
Further reading

APA (2020). Publication manual of the American Psychological Association (7th ed.). The authoritative reference for reporting style.
Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155–159.
Cumming, G. (2014). The new statistics: Why and how. Psychological Science, 25(1), 7–29. — The case for estimation + CIs over NHST-only reporting.
Lakens, D. (2022). Sample size justification. Collabra: Psychology, 8(1). — Modern thinking on effect-size benchmarks and what counts as a meaningful effect.
DSUJ Chapter 3 — The Phoenix of Statistics — covers effect sizes, Bayesian alternatives, and reporting in depth.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text: JASP is free, open-source statistical software developed at the University of Amsterdam. It is designed to make statistical analyses intuitive and interactive — output updates in real time as you change settings.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Download and install JASP
Go to jasp-stats.org/download and download the installer for your operating system (Windows, macOS, or Linux). Installation is straightforward; no licence is required.






TipWhich version?



Always download the latest stable release. If you are working on a university computer that already has JASP installed, make sure it is reasonably recent — some modules used on this site require a recent version.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Loading data into JASP
JASP can open several file formats: .jasp (JASP’s own format), .csv, .sav (SPSS), .xlsx, .ods, and more.

A .jasp file contains both the dataset and any pre-configured analyses, so opening one drops you straight into the analysis. Double-click it or use File → Open.
A .csv / .sav / .xlsx file opens as a plain spreadsheet — after opening, check that every variable has the correct measurement level (the small icon next to the column header).
A dataset from the built-in Data Library — JASP ships with a curated set of practice datasets organised by topic. File → Open → Data Library lets you browse and load any of them in one click; each comes with a short description.








The File panel. The left column has the standard Open / Save / Save As / Export actions; the Data Library row in the middle column reveals JASP’s bundled practice datasets, grouped by analysis topic.


→ ▶ Loading Data (JASP Video Library, ~1 min) walks through opening a file and inspecting it.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
The JASP interface
When you open a dataset, the JASP window is divided into three panels:







Panel
What it shows




Left — Data
The spreadsheet with your variables and cases


Middle — Analysis menu
The list of available statistical tests


Right — Output
Results that update in real time as you configure the analysis










The data-editor toolbar. Analyses flips back to the analysis view, Synchronisation controls whether JASP re-reads the underlying file on disk, Resize Data, Insert and Remove rows/columns, and Undo/Redo the last data edits.



Variable types
JASP recognises three measurement levels (DSUJ §4.6.2). The icon next to each column header tells you which is currently assigned; click it to change.

 Nominal — unordered categories (e.g. treatment group, gender identity).
 Ordinal — ordered categories with no guaranteed equal spacing (e.g. Likert items if you want to be conservative, education level, satisfaction rating).
 Scale (continuous / interval / ratio) — numeric measurements with meaningful spacing (e.g. reaction time, exam score, IQ).

The measurement level matters because JASP uses it to determine which analyses become available, how to dummy-code in regression, and how to render plots. Each variable also carries a value (what JASP computes on) and a label (what the output shows) — nominal and ordinal variables can have non-numeric labels, but scale variables need numeric values.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Editing and preparing your data
A surprising amount of time in any analysis goes into data preparation. JASP’s data editor handles most of what you’d otherwise use SPSS Transform or R dplyr for, including:

Categories: rename, reorder, reverse-code



Task
JASP Video Library




Rename a category’s label (e.g. “1” → “control”)
▶ Renaming Categories


Reorder ordinal categories
▶ Reordering Categories


Add a missing category level
▶ Add Levels


Reverse-code a Likert item (single)
▶ Reverse Code Single


Reverse-code several items at once
▶ Reverse Code Multiple





Variable transformations



Task
JASP Video Library




Compute a new column from an existing one
▶ Compute New Column


Conditional recode (if/else logic)
▶ Use If Else


Centre / standardise a continuous variable (z-scores)
▶ Center Variable


Convert a continuous variable to its ranks
▶ Convert to Ranks





Sum scores and row-level summaries
Aggregating multiple items into a single scale score (e.g. summing the seven items of a depression questionnaire) is a one-step drag-and-drop in JASP, with R-code alternatives for reproducibility:



Task
JASP Video Library




Row sum (sum scores) — drag and drop
▶ Drag and Drop RowSum


Row mean (averaged scale score) — drag and drop
▶ Drag and Drop RowMean


Same, scripted in R
▶ R RowSum · ▶ R RowMean





Missing values
JASP automatically treats the strings NA, NaN, nan, and . as missing (DSUJ §4.6.3). Empty cells also count as missing. If your dataset uses a sentinel value like 999 or -99 for missing, declare it: open the variable’s settings, go to the Missing values tab, tick Custom missing values, and enter the sentinel. JASP will then exclude those rows from analyses that involve the variable, instead of treating them as real data.







The variable settings panel. The Label editor tab (shown) lets you rename categories and toggle the Filter checkbox; the Missing values tab adjacent to it is where you declare custom missing-value codes.








WarningDon’t leave coded-missing values undeclared



Forgetting to declare 999 as missing is one of the most common silent errors in psychology data. The analysis runs, the numbers look fine, but every mean is inflated by the un-recoded missing values. Always inspect the Valid column in Descriptives against the dataset’s row count — they should match if there’s no missingness.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Filtering data
Use a filter when you want to run analyses on a subset of cases — e.g. only female participants, only the within-budget condition, only respondents above a threshold (DSUJ §4.6.4). JASP has three ways:

Tick boxes on a categorical variable — open Variable settings, switch to the Filter column, untick categories you want to exclude.
Drag-and-drop filter builder — click the funnel (▼) icon at the top-left of the data window to open a graphical filter. Drag a variable across, pick an operator (>, <, ==, !=, …), enter a value, and apply.
R code — for more complex conditions (e.g. interactions of multiple variables), the same dialog has an R-code field. Useful when you want the filter logic to live alongside the dataset for reproducibility.




Task
JASP Video Library




Quick filter by category
▶ Filter by Group


Drag-and-drop filter (categorical)
▶ Filter Drag and Drop Categorical


Drag-and-drop filter (continuous)
▶ Filter Drag and Drop Scale



JASP greys out filtered-out rows in the data view, and adds a small filter icon next to the column doing the filtering — so you always know what’s active.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Modules
JASP’s analyses are organised into modules. The basic modules (Descriptives, T-Tests, ANOVA, Regression, Frequencies, Factor) are enabled by default. For everything else — SEM, PROCESS, Network, Meta-Analysis, Bayesian extensions, Machine Learning — you enable the module first.
To install a module: click the blue + icon in the top-right of the JASP window. The module list appears; toggle on the ones you need. Enabled modules appear as new entries in the analysis menu bar.
→ ▶ Enable R Module shows the workflow, using the R-scripting module as an example.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Running an analysis

Select the relevant menu item (e.g., T-Tests → Independent Samples T-Test).
Drag variables into the appropriate boxes (dependent variable, grouping variable).
Tick the options you need (effect size, assumption checks, plots).
Output appears immediately in the right panel.








Anatomy of a typical analysis dialog (Descriptive Statistics shown). The list on the left is your dataset’s variables; arrows move them into the boxes on the right. Output regenerates on the right-hand panel of the main window as soon as you change anything.


There is no “Run” button — JASP updates results live as you click options.






TipNot sure which test to use?



The JASP Video Library has a short ▶ Statistical Test Flowchart that walks through the standard decision tree (data type, number of groups, paired vs independent, etc.).

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Saving your work

File → Save writes a .jasp file containing the data, all current analyses, and their settings — open it again later and pick up where you left off.
To export a single result table or figure, click the downward triangle (▼) next to the result and choose Copy, Save as image, or similar.
To export everything as a report, File → Export Results writes an HTML or PDF report of the full output.

text:
Ready to start?
Head to Descriptive Statistics to begin exploring your data, or jump to any topic in the navigation bar.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Advanced Topics
Missing data is almost inevitable in psychology research. How you handle it matters: the wrong approach can introduce bias and reduce statistical power. This page covers the types of missingness and the options available for dealing with it.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Types of missingness
Before choosing a method, you need to think about why data are missing.








Mechanism
Definition
Example




MCAR — Missing Completely At Random
The probability of being missing is unrelated to any variable (observed or unobserved)
A random equipment failure causes some data to be lost


MAR — Missing At Random
The probability of being missing depends on observed variables, but not on the missing value itself
Older participants more likely to skip a question about technology use, but we observe age


MNAR — Missing Not At Random
The probability of being missing depends on the missing value itself (or unobserved variables)
Participants with the highest depression scores are most likely to drop out of a depression study









WarningWhy this matters




MCAR: listwise deletion is unbiased (but wasteful)
MAR: listwise deletion is biased. Multiple imputation or FIML is appropriate
MNAR: all standard methods can produce biased results. Requires sensitivity analysis or specialised models.



In practice, MCAR is often implausible — assume MAR unless you have strong reasons otherwise.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
section: How JASP handles missing data right now
text:
How JASP handles missing data right now
JASP currently offers two deletion-based approaches across the standard analysis modules.

Listwise deletion (complete-case analysis) — the JASP default
Only cases with complete data on all variables involved in the analysis are included.

When it is acceptable: MCAR (rare in practice), or MAR with a small proportion of missingness
Problem: throws away information from partially complete cases; biased under MAR; reduces sample size and statistical power



Pairwise deletion — available in some analyses (e.g., correlation matrices)
Each pairwise statistic uses all cases that have valid data on the two variables involved. Different cells in a correlation matrix can therefore be based on different n.

Problem: can produce inconsistent estimates and occasionally yields correlation matrices that are not positive-definite — which then break downstream procedures like regression or factor analysis



What about mean substitution?
Replacing missing values with the variable mean distorts the distribution, shrinks variance, and biases correlations. Don’t do it. JASP does not offer it as a default option, and it should not be re-introduced manually.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
section: What JASP does not currently do
text:
What JASP does not currently do

Multiple Imputation (MI)
MI is the recommended approach for MAR data in modern guidance — but it is not yet available in JASP. An integration of the R package mice is in active development, but has not been released in the stable version of JASP at the time of writing. For now, MI has to be done outside JASP — in R (mice, Amelia), SPSS, or Mplus — and the pooled results reported manually.
What MI does, conceptually: it creates m complete datasets (typically 20–100) by drawing plausible values for each missing cell from a model of the observed data; each dataset is analysed separately, and estimates are combined using Rubin’s rules to give a single set of coefficients, standard errors, and p-values that correctly reflects the uncertainty introduced by missingness.


Little’s MCAR test
Not currently in JASP. Available in SPSS, or in R via naniar::mcar_test().

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
FIML in structural-equation models
JASP’s structural-equation modelling (SEM) module is built on lavaan, which supports Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) estimation under MAR. If you are fitting an SEM (path model, latent variable model, CFA), FIML is the preferred way to handle missing data: it uses all available information without imputing values. Set the missing-data option in the SEM analysis options.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Diagnosing missingness in JASP

Descriptives: the Valid column shows the number of non-missing values per variable. Compare to your total N to see where the gaps are.
By group: split descriptives by a grouping variable to see whether missingness clusters in one group.
Detailed patterns: for missing-value heatmaps or upset plots (which cases miss which combinations of variables), export the dataset to R and use naniar or VIM.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Reporting






TipReporting missing data



State (a) the percentage of missing data per variable, (b) the missingness mechanism you assume (MCAR/MAR/MNAR) and why, and (c) the method used to handle it.
Example: “Data were missing on [variable] for 12% of participants. Missingness appeared unrelated to observed study variables (assessed by comparing complete and incomplete cases on demographic and outcome variables), so listwise deletion was used.”
If you used multiple imputation outside JASP, also report m (the number of imputations), the imputation model (variables included), and the software used (e.g., R mice 3.x).

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Further reading

Van Buuren, S. (2018). Flexible Imputation of Missing Data (2nd ed.). CRC Press. — Freely available at stefvanbuuren.name/fimd/
Sterne, J. A. C., et al. (2009). Multiple imputation for missing data in epidemiological and clinical research. BMJ, 338, b2393.
DSUJ Chapter 6 — Bias — includes discussion of data problems including missingness

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
Meta-analysis is a statistical method for synthesising effect sizes across multiple studies. Rather than relying on a single study, a meta-analysis pools estimates to arrive at a more precise, generalisable conclusion about an effect.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
When to use meta-analysis

You have collected effect sizes (or sufficient statistics) from multiple independent studies on the same research question
Your thesis includes a systematic review with a quantitative synthesis component
You want to quantify heterogeneity — do effect sizes vary across studies, and if so, why?

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Key concepts

Effect size metrics
Choose the effect size metric appropriate for your data:



Data type
Effect size metric




Two group means (continuous DV)
Cohen’s d / Hedges’ g


Correlational data
Fisher’s z-transformed r


Frequencies / proportions
Log odds ratio (log OR)


Means across time / conditions
Standardised mean change





Fixed-effects vs. random-effects models








Model
Assumption
Use when




Fixed-effects
All studies estimate the same true effect
Exact replications — rarely appropriate


Random-effects
Studies estimate different true effects drawn from a distribution
Different studies, different populations — almost always preferred



In a random-effects model, the variance of the distribution of true effects (τ²) is estimated. A larger τ² indicates more heterogeneity.


Heterogeneity







Statistic
What it tells you




Q
Total variability in effect sizes; significant Q indicates heterogeneity beyond sampling error


I²
Percentage of total variability due to true heterogeneity (not sampling error). I² = 25% (low), 50% (moderate), 75% (high) as rough guides


τ (tau)
The standard deviation of the distribution of true effects — in the original effect size metric

title: Meta-Analysis
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Install the Meta-Analysis module: click the blue + icon in the top-right of the JASP window to open the module list, then enable Meta-Analysis.
The JASP Meta-Analysis module is a graphical interface to the R package metafor (Viechtbauer, 2010), with additional post-processing on top. Behind the scenes, every analysis maps to a metafor call, which means you get the full state-of-the-art set of methods — but through a point-and-click interface (Bartoš, Wagenmakers & Viechtbauer, 2025).
The typical workflow has three stages: (1) compute effect sizes from the raw study results, (2) inspect the data with a funnel plot, (3) fit a meta-analytic model and visualise it with a forest plot. Each stage is its own analysis under the Meta-Analysis menu.

Stage 1 — Compute effect sizes from raw data
Open Meta-Analysis → Effect Size Computation. In the Design, Measurement, and Effect size dropdowns, pick the configuration matching your data (e.g. Independent groups, Binary, RR for risk ratios; or Independent groups, Continuous, SMD for standardised mean differences). Then drag the relevant outcome and group-size columns into the boxes. The output panel reports how many effect sizes were computed successfully.







The Effect Size Computation dialog on the BCG-vaccine dataset: thirteen trials reporting positive (tpos) and negative (tneg) tuberculosis cases in vaccinated and control groups. JASP computes the log risk ratio and standard error for each study and stores them as new columns in the data.


This stage calls metafor::escalc() behind the scenes — every effect-size choice that escalc supports (RR, OR, RD, SMD, ROM, ZCOR, PR, IR, …) is available in the dropdown. Chain multiple computations with the green + button if you have several outcomes.


Stage 2 — Inspect the data with a funnel plot
Open Meta-Analysis → Funnel Plot. Drag the computed effect-size and standard-error columns into the input panel. JASP draws each study as a point in effect-size × standard-error space, with the funnel representing the sampling distribution under the chosen null. Asymmetry around the centre line is the visual hint of publication bias (formal tests live in the Publication Bias / Sensitivity Analyses section — see below).


Stage 3 — Fit a meta-analytic model and draw a forest plot
Open Meta-Analysis → Classical Meta-Analysis. Drag Effect Size and Effect Size Standard Error into the data inputs. The defaults follow metafor recommendations: REML for the random-effects variance, and the Knapp–Hartung small-sample adjustment for the fixed-effects test (Knapp & Hartung, 2003). Tick Forest Plot to visualise; the figure renders alongside the pooled estimate, heterogeneity statistics, and the underlying metafor R code for reproducibility.







The Classical Meta-Analysis output for the BCG vaccine data. The model summary (top right) gives a pooled risk ratio of 0.49 with heterogeneity Q(12) = 152.23, p < .001. The forest plot (bottom right) shows each of the 13 trials, the diamond at the bottom is the pooled effect, and JASP exposes the metafor code at the bottom for export to R if needed.




Beyond the basics — meta-regression and moderators
Drag one or more variables into the Predictors box of the Classical Meta-Analysis dialog to fit a meta-regression — the same model, but with covariates explaining between-study heterogeneity. Tick Estimated Marginal Means and Contrasts to get marginal means at specific predictor levels, and tick Bubble Plot to visualise the meta-regression slope:







A meta-regression on the writing-to-learn dataset (Example 2 of Bartoš et al., 2025) with two predictors. The output gives an omnibus test for each predictor (top right), individual coefficients, marginal-mean tables across levels of feedback, and a bubble plot of effect size against length with study weights encoded by bubble size.




Advanced models (multilevel, multivariate, cluster-robust SEs)
For nested data (effect sizes within studies within research groups), open the Random Effects / Model Components section in the Classical Meta-Analysis dialog and add a Nested (multilevel) component, specifying the grouping variables at each level. For dependent effect sizes (e.g. several outcomes per study), the Effect Size Variance-Covariance Matrix analysis lets you specify the dependency structure that goes into the model. Both surfaces also support cluster-robust standard errors with one tickbox.
These advanced models put JASP roughly on par with metafor itself for what’s expressible through the GUI — multilevel, multivariate, location–scale meta-regression, and small-sample corrections are all accessible without writing a single line of R.






NoteJASP Video Library — Meta-Analysis



Short walkthrough recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Meta-Analysis.html:

▶ Effect Size Computation — computing d, r, or log OR from raw statistics
▶ Main Analysis — random-effects model, forest plot, heterogeneity statistics

For the full guided walkthrough on real datasets (BCG vaccines, writing-to-learn interventions, recidivism), see Bartoš, F., Wagenmakers, E.-J., & Viechtbauer, W. (2025). Meta-Analysis with JASP, Part I: Classical Approaches. arXiv:2509.09845. Annotated JASP files: osf.io/mb56t.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Reading a forest plot
A forest plot shows:

Each study as a horizontal line: the point estimate (square) and its confidence interval (the line)
The size of the square is proportional to the study’s weight (usually inverse variance)
The diamond at the bottom shows the pooled effect and its CI
The dashed vertical line is the pooled effect; the solid vertical line at 0 (or 1 for ORs) is the null

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Publication bias
Studies with null results are less likely to be published, which can inflate meta-analytic estimates. Assess with:

Funnel plot: plot effect size vs. standard error — asymmetry suggests publication bias
Egger’s test: a regression test for funnel plot asymmetry
Trim-and-fill method: imputes “missing” studies to correct for asymmetry

All of these are available in JASP’s meta-analysis module.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Moderator analysis
If I² is high (> 50%), explore moderators — study-level variables (e.g., participant age, intervention type, publication year) that might explain heterogeneity. In JASP, add these as covariates in a meta-regression.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Interpreting the output






TipAPA reporting example



A random-effects meta-analysis of k = 18 studies (total N = 2,341) yielded a pooled effect of d = 0.43, 95% CI [0.28, 0.58], z = 5.60, p < .001. Heterogeneity was moderate, Q(17) = 34.2, p = .007, I² = 50%. There was no evidence of publication bias based on Egger’s test (t(16) = 1.21, p = .24).

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Further reading

Borenstein, M., Hedges, L. V., Higgins, J. P. T., & Rothstein, H. R. (2009). Introduction to meta-analysis. Wiley. — The standard text.
Cochrane Handbook — comprehensive guide for systematic reviews in health research.
Viechtbauer, W. (2010). Conducting meta-analyses in R with the metafor package. Journal of Statistical Software, 36(3), 1–48. — The R package JASP’s Meta-Analysis module builds on.
Bartoš, F., Wagenmakers, E.-J., & Viechtbauer, W. (2025). Meta-analysis with JASP, Part I: Classical approaches. arXiv:2509.09845. — End-to-end tutorial with three worked examples (BCG vaccines, writing-to-learn, recidivism) covering effect-size computation through multilevel and multivariate models. Annotated JASP files at osf.io/mb56t.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Try it yourself
Meta-analysis is not part of the DSUJ Labcoat Leni / Smart Alex companion set, so there is no pre-packaged worked-solution dataset on discoverjasp. The examples below use the metafor package’s bundled datasets, which JASP can import via the CSV/RData routes.

Exercise — Pool the BCG vaccine trials
The classic meta-analysis teaching dataset is dat.bcg from the R package metafor — 13 trials of the BCG vaccine against tuberculosis with binary outcomes. Export it from R with metafor::dat.bcg and write.csv(metafor::dat.bcg, "bcg.csv"), then import into JASP. Fit a random-effects meta-analysis on the log risk ratios, request a forest plot, and inspect the heterogeneity statistics (Q and I²).






Note▸ Worked walkthrough






In R / metafor: the dat.bcg example is documented in the metafor analysis examples (the random-effects model with log RR is the standard demonstration).
In JASP: the JASP Video Library — Meta-Analysis shows the equivalent workflow end-to-end.






Exercise — Moderator analysis on the same data
Using the same dataset, add ablat (absolute latitude of the trial) as a moderator. Does latitude explain a significant portion of the between-study heterogeneity? Reproduce the classic result that vaccine effectiveness increases with latitude.






Note▸ Worked walkthrough






metafor users can fit this as rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ ablat, data = dat.bcg, method = "REML") and inspect the moderator’s p-value, R², and residual heterogeneity. JASP’s Meta-Analysis module exposes the same model via the Moderators input.

title: Network Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
Psychometric network analysis models psychological constructs not as reflections of a single underlying latent variable, but as systems of directly interacting components. Instead of asking “is there a common factor that explains why these symptoms covary?”, the network approach asks “how do these symptoms interact with one another to produce the observed covariation?”. For depression: rather than five items reflecting a single “depression” factor, insomnia might directly cause fatigue, fatigue might directly cause concentration problems, and so on (Borsboom & Cramer, 2013; Isvoranu et al., 2022).

title: Network Analysis
section: The basic object: a pairwise Markov random field (PMRF)
text:
The basic object: a pairwise Markov random field (PMRF)
In a PMRF — the workhorse of network psychometrics — nodes represent variables (typically scale items or symptoms) and edges represent the strength of conditional association between two variables after controlling for all other variables in the network. An edge is present between two nodes only if the corresponding variables are conditionally dependent given the rest; an absent edge encodes conditional independence (Isvoranu et al., 2022, Ch 6).
This is the key distinction from a correlation matrix: in a PMRF, a connection from A to B survives only after partialling out every other variable in the system. The remaining edge therefore represents a unique pairwise association — not driven by some third variable elsewhere in the network.








Data type
Standard PMRF
What edges encode




Continuous
Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM)
Partial correlations, bounded between −1 and 1


Binary
Ising model
Log-linear interaction parameters


Mixed (continuous + categorical + count)
Mixed Graphical Model (MGM)
Regression coefficients from generalised linear models



In all three, an edge-weight of 0 means conditional independence; magnitudes encode strength.

title: Network Analysis
section: Why estimating networks is harder than estimating correlations
text:
Why estimating networks is harder than estimating correlations
If you naively estimate every pairwise partial correlation in a moderately sized network, you’ll overfit. With p variables there are p(p − 1)/2 potential edges, and even pure-noise data will yield many small-but-nonzero estimates. The estimated network will look dense and uninterpretable, and won’t replicate.
Network psychometrics handles this with model selection / sparsification. Isvoranu et al. (Ch 7) describe four families:








Family
Idea
Common implementation




Thresholding
Estimate all edges, then set those below some cutoff (e.g. p > .05 or
edge


Pruning
Re-estimate the model after removing non-significant edges
bootnet post-estimation step


Model search
Compare many candidate sparse networks and pick the best by an information criterion
IsingFit, custom search


Regularisation
Penalise edge weights toward zero during estimation; the strongest edges survive
EBIC graphical LASSO — the default in qgraph and bootnet



The most widely used pipeline in psychology — and the one JASP’s network module is built on — is the EBIC graphical LASSO for GGM. The LASSO shrinks small partial correlations to exactly zero; the EBIC (Extended Bayesian Information Criterion; Chen & Chen, 2008) chooses the tuning parameter γ that balances fit and sparsity. Higher γ (commonly 0.5) yields sparser, more conservative networks.






TipWhat is “regularisation”, in one paragraph?





The standard partial-correlation estimate from a sample maximises the likelihood of the data. Regularised estimation maximises the likelihood penalised by the sum of |edge weights|. Edges whose true value is close to zero collapse to exactly zero rather than wobbling around it. The cost: edges whose true value is small but nonzero get underestimated. The benefit: the estimated network is sparser, more interpretable, and replicates better.

title: Network Analysis
text:
Centrality: which nodes matter most?
A central question in network analysis is which nodes play a special role in the system. Three commonly reported centrality metrics (Isvoranu et al., Ch 3):








Metric
What it captures
Caveat




Strength (or node strength)
Sum of absolute edge weights touching the node — direct connectivity
Most stable; recommended as the default centrality measure


Closeness
Inverse of the mean shortest-path length from the node to every other — how quickly information can “spread” from this node
Less stable in small/noisy networks; interpret with caution


Betweenness
The number of shortest paths between other nodes that pass through this node — “bridge” role
Even less stable; recent work (Bringmann et al., 2019; Isvoranu et al., Ch 3) warns against over-reliance









WarningCentrality is not the same as causal importance



A node high on strength is statistically central in the estimated network — many edges touch it. That is not the same as “intervening on this node will move the rest of the system”. Treat centrality as descriptive of the estimated network, not as a recipe for treatment targeting (Bringmann et al., 2019; Dablander & Hinne, 2019).

title: Network Analysis
text:
How stable is your network?
Network estimates have sampling variability — the same population could yield a fairly different estimated network in another sample. Reporting a single point estimate without quantifying uncertainty is the network-analysis equivalent of reporting a p-value without an effect size. Isvoranu et al. (Ch 8) and Epskamp, Borsboom & Fried (2018) describe the standard checks, all implemented in bootnet:

Bootstrap edge weights — produces a confidence interval around each edge weight. Edges whose CI crosses zero shouldn’t be over-interpreted, even if they survived regularisation.
Bootstrap difference tests — for any two edges (or two centralities), does the bootstrap support the conclusion that one is larger than the other? Often surprisingly few pairs differ reliably.
Case-dropping subset bootstrap — refit the network on subsets of cases and compute the CS-coefficient for each centrality metric. A CS coefficient < 0.25 means that centrality is not stable enough to interpret; > 0.5 is considered acceptable.
Network Comparison Test (NCT) — when comparing two networks (e.g. before vs after treatment, depressed vs control), tests whether they differ in overall structure or in specific edges (van Borkulo et al., 2022).

title: Network Analysis
text:
Beyond cross-sectional GGM
Most of what’s described above applies to a single cross-section. Isvoranu et al. (Parts III–IV) discuss extensions for longitudinal and dynamic settings:

Graphical Vector Auto-Regression (GVAR) — for N = 1 time series or panel data; separates contemporaneous and temporal (lagged) effects.
Multi-level GVAR — pooled across multiple participants’ time series.
Time-varying network models — allows the network structure itself to change over time.
Ising-model simulations — using estimated Ising parameters to simulate how the system would behave dynamically; useful for hypothesis generation.

These methods sit outside JASP’s current network module and require R (graphicalVAR, mlVAR, tvNet).

title: Network Analysis
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP

JASP has a Network analysis module that wraps qgraph / bootnet for cross-sectional GGM (and Ising for binary data). The typical workflow once the module is enabled:

Select your indicator variables (the questionnaire items or symptoms).
Choose the estimator — EBIC graphical LASSO is the standard for continuous data; IsingFit for binary. 
Set the EBIC tuning parameter γ (typically 0.5; values between 0 and 0.5 are common).
Plot the network (qgraph rendering) — JASP lets you adjust the layout (Fruchterman-Reingold is the default), edge transparency, and node grouping.
Request centrality plots — strength, closeness, betweenness, expected influence.
Request stability checks — bootstrapped edge CIs and CS-coefficients for centrality.

For longitudinal / multi-level / temporal networks, JASP does not currently have built-in tooling; switch to R.

title: Network Analysis
text:
Interpreting and reporting






TipReporting a network analysis



Following the consensus reporting standards (Burger et al., 2023; Epskamp & Fried, 2018), at minimum report:

The sample: N, demographics, how it was recruited.
The items / variables that made up the nodes, and how they were scored.
The estimator used (e.g. “GGM estimated with the graphical LASSO, tuning parameter selected by EBIC with γ = 0.5”) and the software / package version.
The estimated network: a figure showing nodes, edges, and edge thickness, with a clear legend. Either share the partial-correlation matrix or the figure source.
Centrality estimates, with both the values and a stability check (CS-coefficient).
Bootstrap diagnostics: edge confidence intervals and (where relevant) edge-difference and centrality-difference tests.
Substantive interpretation, tempered by what the stability analysis can actually support.









WarningThree common over-interpretation pitfalls




“This node is most central, so it is most important to treat.” → Centrality describes the estimated network; whether intervening will move the system is a separate claim that the network alone cannot support.
“The network shows that depression is caused by insomnia.” → Cross-sectional PMRFs describe conditional dependence patterns, not causal direction. Use longitudinal designs for directional claims.
“The two networks are different.” → Two networks can look qualitatively different but pass a formal Network Comparison Test as statistically indistinguishable. Always test the comparison formally before claiming difference.

title: Network Analysis
text:
Further reading

Isvoranu, A.-M., Epskamp, S., Waldorp, L. J., & Borsboom, D. (Eds.). (2022). Network psychometrics with R: A guide for behavioral and social scientists. Routledge. — Comprehensive treatment of cross-sectional GGM/Ising/MGM, longitudinal extensions, and the causal/theoretical underpinnings.
Borsboom, D., & Cramer, A. O. J. (2013). Network analysis: An integrative approach to the structure of psychopathology. Annual Review of Clinical Psychology, 9, 91–121. — The classic theoretical paper.
Epskamp, S., & Fried, E. I. (2018). A tutorial on regularized partial correlation networks. Psychological Methods, 23(4), 617–634. — The most widely cited applied tutorial.
Epskamp, S., Borsboom, D., & Fried, E. I. (2018). Estimating psychological networks and their accuracy: A tutorial paper. Behavior Research Methods, 50, 195–212. — Detailed walkthrough of bootnet stability checks.
Bringmann, L. F., Elmer, T., Epskamp, S., Krause, R. W., Schoch, D., Wichers, M., Wigman, J. T. W., & Snippe, E. (2019). What do centrality measures measure in psychological networks? Journal of Abnormal Psychology, 128(8), 892–903. — Why “central node = important node” is more contested than it seems.
Dablander, F., & Hinne, M. (2019). Node centrality measures are a poor substitute for causal inference. Scientific Reports, 9, 6846. — Centrality describes structure; it does not identify intervention targets.
van Borkulo, C. D., van Bork, R., Boschloo, L., Kossakowski, J. J., Tio, P., Schoevers, R. A., Borsboom, D., & Waldorp, L. J. (2022). Comparing network structures on three aspects: A permutation test. Psychological Methods, 28(6), 1273–1285. — The Network Comparison Test (NCT).
Chen, J., & Chen, Z. (2008). Extended Bayesian information criteria for model selection with large model spaces. Biometrika, 95(3), 759–771. — Source of the EBIC used in graphical-LASSO model selection.
Burger, J., Isvoranu, A.-M., Lunansky, G., Haslbeck, J. M. B., Epskamp, S., Hoekstra, R. H. A., Fried, E. I., Borsboom, D., & Blanken, T. F. (2023). Reporting standards for psychological network analyses in cross-sectional data. Psychological Methods, 28(4), 806–824. — Current reporting checklist for cross-sectional networks.

title: Network Analysis
text:
R packages worth knowing







Package
Purpose




qgraph
Network visualisation and basic GGM estimation


bootnet
One-stop wrapper for estimation + stability checks


IsingFit
Ising-model estimation for binary data


mgm
Mixed Graphical Model estimation


graphicalVAR, mlVAR
Temporal / longitudinal network models


NetworkComparisonTest
Formal between-network comparison (van Borkulo et al.)

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
Factor analysis is a family of techniques for identifying latent dimensions (factors) that underlie a set of observed variables. It is widely used in scale development, construct validation, and data reduction.
Three related techniques are covered here:

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA)

When to use it

You have a set of items (e.g., questionnaire items) and you do not know — or want to discover — how many factors underlie them
Scale development: determining the dimensional structure of a new measure
Generating hypotheses about construct structure to be tested later with CFA



Key decisions
How many factors?

Scree plot: look for the “elbow” — the point where the eigenvalues flatten out
Kaiser criterion: retain factors with eigenvalue > 1 (can over-extract in large datasets)
Parallel analysis: compare actual eigenvalues to those from random data — more accurate than the Kaiser criterion (recommended)

Rotation method
Rotation makes factors more interpretable. Two families:








Family
Method
Use when




Orthogonal
Varimax
You expect factors to be uncorrelated


Oblique
Oblimin / Promax
You expect factors to correlate (common in psychology)



In psychology, oblique rotation is usually more realistic.
What loadings tell you
A factor loading is the correlation between an item and a factor. Loadings > .30 (or .40 for a more conservative threshold) are typically considered meaningful. An item should load strongly on one factor and minimally on others — check for cross-loadings.


How to do it in JASP
Go to Factor → Exploratory Factor Analysis.






NoteJASP Video Library — EFA



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Factor-Analysis.html#efa:

▶ EFA Main Analysis — number of factors, rotation method, factor loadings, scree plot

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA)

When to use it

You have a theoretically specified model of which items load on which factors
Testing whether a previously validated scale replicates in your sample
Comparing alternative factor structures (e.g., one-factor vs. two-factor)
As a prerequisite to testing measurement invariance across groups



Model fit indices








Index
Acceptable range
Notes




χ²
Non-significant (p > .05)
Very sensitive to sample size — rarely met with large N


CFI / TLI
> .90 (acceptable), > .95 (good)
Comparative Fit Index / Tucker-Lewis Index


RMSEA
< .08 (acceptable), < .05 (good)
Root Mean Square Error of Approximation


SRMR
< .08
Standardised Root Mean Square Residual









TipAlways report multiple fit indices



No single fit index is sufficient. Report CFI, TLI, RMSEA (with 90% CI), and SRMR together.




How to do it in JASP
Go to Factor → Confirmatory Factor Analysis.






NoteJASP Video Library — CFA



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Factor-Analysis.html#cfa:

▶ CFA Main Analysis — specifying a CFA model, fit indices, modification indices

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

When to use it

You want to reduce a large number of variables to a smaller set of components for use in further analyses
You want to remove multicollinearity by replacing correlated variables with orthogonal components
You are not primarily interested in a latent variable model



How to do it in JASP
Go to Factor → Principal Component Analysis.






NoteJASP Video Library — PCA



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Factor-Analysis.html#pca:

▶ PCA Main Analysis — number of components, rotation, component loadings and scores

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Reliability: Cronbach’s α
When developing or using a scale, report Cronbach’s α as a measure of internal consistency. Values > .70 are conventionally considered acceptable.
Cronbach’s α is available in JASP under Factor → Reliability Analysis. Note that α depends on the number of items and their average inter-correlation, so it is not a pure measure of unidimensionality.

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Further reading

DSUJ chapter on factor analysis (check your edition) — EFA and CFA in JASP with worked examples.
Brown, T. A. (2015). Confirmatory factor analysis for applied research (2nd ed.). Guilford Press. — The applied CFA standard; chapters 1–5 cover the EFA/CFA distinction, model specification, fit, and revision.
Kline, R. B. (2023). Principles and practice of structural equation modeling (5th ed.). Guilford Press. — Chapter 14 gives a compact CFA treatment in the broader SEM context.
For measurement equivalence across groups, see the Measurement Invariance page.

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Try it yourself
The DSUJ companion site (Labcoat Leni / Smart Alex) does not include a packaged factor-analysis worked solution at the time of writing, so the exercise below uses a smaller JASP demo dataset and points to the rendered output as a check.

Exercise — Explore the latent structure of an exam-anxiety dataset
Using exam_anxiety.jasp, inspect the correlation matrix among the continuous variables. Then fit an EFA and decide on the number of factors using the parallel-analysis option and the scree plot. Finally, fit a single-factor CFA and inspect the fit indices.






Note▸ Annotated DSUJ output






exam_anxiety.html — the DSUJ-rendered output for this dataset (descriptives and correlations; not a full factor-analysis walkthrough).










TipFor more substantial practice



A genuine multi-item scale is more informative than a four-variable demo. The Big Five Inventory (50 Likert items, freely available from the Open Psychometrics Project) is a classic worked example: import the CSV into JASP, fit an EFA with five factors and oblimin rotation, and inspect whether the resulting loadings recover the OCEAN dimensions.

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Advanced Topics
Mediation and moderation both involve a third variable in the relationship between a predictor (X) and an outcome (Y) — but they ask fundamentally different questions.
In JASP, both are estimated using the PROCESS module, developed by Andrew Hayes.

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Mediation

What is it?
Mediation tests whether the effect of X on Y is transmitted through a third variable M (the mediator).
X → M → Y      (indirect effect)
X → Y          (direct effect, controlling for M)
The total effect of X on Y is decomposed into:

Direct effect: the effect of X on Y after accounting for M
Indirect effect (a × b): the effect of X on Y that goes through M (= path a × path b)







TipDecomposing the total effect





The trick of mediation: the original X → Y relationship gets split into two pathways. The direct effect (X → Y, controlling for M) and the indirect effect (X → M → Y). A relationship that’s completely mediated by M tells a very different psychological story than one with no mediation at all.





Assumptions


The causal ordering X → M → Y must be theoretically justified — mediation analysis does not prove causality
All variables are measured without error (impossible in practice, but a key limitation to acknowledge)
There is no unmeasured confounding of the X → M, M → Y, or X → Y relationships
No interaction between X and M — if you suspect one, use moderated mediation (Hayes Model 8 or 14)




Inference: bootstrapping
The indirect effect (a × b) is non-normally distributed. JASP uses bootstrapped confidence intervals (typically 5000–10000 samples). An indirect effect is considered significant if the 95% CI does not contain zero.


How to do it in JASP
Install the PROCESS module: click the blue + icon in the top-right of the JASP window to open the module list, then enable PROCESS.






NoteJASP Video Library — Mediation



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_PROCESS.html#mediation:

▶ Load Module — installing and opening the PROCESS module
▶ Specify Model — assigning X, M, and Y; bootstrapped indirect effects
▶ Hayes Model — using pre-defined Hayes templates (Model 4 for simple mediation)





Interpreting mediation output







Path
What to look for




Path a
X → M: is the predictor associated with the mediator?


Path b
M → Y: does the mediator predict the outcome, controlling for X?


Direct effect (c’)
X → Y controlling for M: has the effect changed from the total effect?


Indirect effect (a × b)
Is the 95% bootstrapped CI excluding zero?



Full mediation: the direct effect becomes non-significant once M is included — X only affects Y through M. Partial mediation: both direct and indirect effects remain significant.






TipAPA reporting



Report the indirect effect with its bootstrapped CI: indirect effect = [value], 95% CI [lower, upper]. For the direct and total effects, report b, SE, t, p.

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Moderation

What is it?
Moderation tests whether the effect of X on Y differs across levels of a third variable W (the moderator). Statistically, this is an interaction effect: you add the product term X × W to the regression model.
If the X × W interaction is significant, the slope of X on Y is different at different values of W.






TipModeration = interaction = “steering”





A useful mnemonic: to moderate means to steer — to control how something unfolds. Statistically, moderation is just an interaction effect: one predictor steers how another predictor affects the outcome. Which of the two predictors gets called “the moderator” is somewhat arbitrary — it’s about which framing matches your theoretical question.





Assumptions


The same as multiple regression — see the Regression page
Centering: for continuous moderators, it is good practice to mean-centre X and W before computing the product term. This reduces multicollinearity and makes the lower-order coefficients interpretable. JASP does this automatically




Simple slopes and interaction plots
After a significant moderation, inspect simple slopes: the slope of X on Y at specific values of W (typically M − 1 SD, M, M + 1 SD for a continuous moderator). JASP provides simple slope plots and significance tests.


How to do it in JASP






NoteJASP Video Library — Moderation



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_PROCESS.html#moderation:

▶ Specify Model — entering X, W, and the X×W product term
▶ Hayes Model 1 — simple moderation using a Hayes template
▶ Flexplot — visualising the interaction
▶ Simple Slopes Plot — effect of X at low/medium/high levels of W

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Moderated mediation
When moderation and mediation are combined — the indirect effect of X on Y through M depends on the level of W — this is moderated mediation (or conditional indirect effect). Hayes’ Model 8 and Model 14 are the most common configurations.
The indirect effect is estimated separately at different values of W. A Johnson-Neyman interval identifies the exact range of W values for which the indirect effect is significant.

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
The PROCESS module up close
Beyond simple mediation and moderation, JASP’s Process module supports general multi-step causal models with several mediators and moderators specified as a DAG. The walkthrough below follows Lüken, Vroegh, Rohrer, van Doorn & Wagenmakers (2024), which uses data from McGarrigle et al. (2021) on listening-related fatigue across the adult lifespan (N = 281). The research question is: does the relationship between age and listening fatigue run through auditory attention, perceived hearing impairment, perceived memory, and mood disturbances?






TipPROCESS in JASP is not the same code as the SPSS PROCESS macro



The JASP module fits PROCESS-style models via structural equation modelling (lavaan under the hood). That means you specify which variables are mediators / moderators explicitly in a DAG, rather than picking a Hayes template number. This forces you to think about which causal structure you’re assuming — and lets you test goodness-of-fit, which the SPSS PROCESS macro does not (Lüken et al., 2024).



Step 1 — Assign variables
After installing the Process (beta) module, open Process → Classic Process Model. Drag the outcome into Dependent Variable and every predictor / mediator / moderator into Continuous Predictors or Categorical Predictors.







Variable assignment in the Process dialog. Fatigue is the outcome; the four candidate mediators (AudAttention, HearImpair, Memory, MoodDisturb) and the focal predictor Age go into Continuous Predictors.




Step 2 — Specify the causal paths
In the Models section, click the green + for each path you want in the model. For every path, pick variables in the From and To dropdowns and a Process Type (Direct, Mediator, or Moderation). The example below codes a multi-mediator model: age has a direct effect on fatigue, plus indirect effects through auditory attention, hearing impairment, and memory.







The causal-graph view of the Lüken et al. (2024) Model 1 — age (left) influences fatigue (right) directly and through three parallel mediators (auditory attention, hearing impairment, memory).




Step 3 — Tick the right options
Under Options, request Bootstrap confidence intervals (the default for indirect effects). To check whether the implied causal model is consistent with the data, tick Local tests under Tests — these test the conditional-independence implications of your DAG, and significant ones flag model misspecification:







Per-model options. Mediation parameter estimates are on by default; tick Local tests under Tests to test the model’s implied conditional independencies.




Step 4 — Read the mediation-effects table
The headline output is the Mediation effects table, which breaks down the total effect of X on Y into the direct effect plus each specific indirect path, with bootstrapped CIs:







Decomposition of the age → fatigue relationship. The direct effect is significant and negative (older adults report less fatigue, holding the mediators constant). Among the indirect paths, only age → HearImpair → Fatigue is significant (95% CI [0.031, 0.115]) — older adults perceive more hearing impairment, which in turn predicts more fatigue.




Comparing competing models
The module supports adding multiple models side-by-side (each with its own causal graph and option panel) and compares them via AIC, BIC, and AIC/BIC weights. In the worked example, the authors add a Model 2 that includes age × MoodDisturb as a moderator on the path to fatigue. AIC/BIC favour the larger model, but the interaction term itself is not significant — reproducing the original McGarrigle et al. finding that mood does not moderate the age-fatigue relationship.


Caveats from the tutorial
Lüken et al. (2024) close with three warnings worth repeating:

The module assumes your DAG is correct. It can test the statistical implications of a specified structure (conditional independencies, fit), but no test can distinguish your structure from one with reversed arrows. Causal-direction claims still need theory or experimental manipulation.
Unobserved confounders bias indirect effects even in experimental designs that manipulate X. PROCESS results are conditional on the no-unobserved-confounders assumption — say so explicitly when reporting.
Don’t chase fit by tweaking the model after seeing local-test results. Modifying the model post-hoc on the basis of failed local tests is the SEM equivalent of HARKing. If the fit is poor, report it, hypothesise an alternative structure, and test that on a different sample.

→ Full tutorial: Lüken, M., Vroegh, T., Rohrer, J. M., van Doorn, J., & Wagenmakers, E.-J. (2024). Causal inference in JASP: The Process module — JASP blog. Walks through the listening-fatigue worked example end-to-end, including the Model 1 vs Model 2 comparison.

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Basics
Regression allows you to model the relationship between a continuous outcome variable (the dependent variable, DV) and one or more predictors (the independent variables, IVs). Start with correlation to quantify the relationship between two variables, then move to regression when you want to predict an outcome or test the unique contribution of each predictor.
This page covers:

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Correlation






TipResearch vignette — which student traits predict the kind of lecturer they like?



Chamorro-Premuzic et al. (2008) measured five Big Five traits in students and five dimensions of their preferred lecturer style (knowledgeable, charismatic, accessible, and so on). The natural first move is a full correlation matrix: every trait against every lecturer dimension, to see which pairs hang together and in what direction. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 7).
→ Worked in Exercise 2 below.



What is it?
A correlation coefficient (r) describes the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two continuous variables. Pearson’s r ranges from −1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive), with 0 indicating no linear relationship.



r
Conventional label




±.10
Small


±.30
Medium


±.50
Large





When to use it

As a descriptive measure of association between two variables (no implied causality)
As a preliminary step before regression
When reporting relationships in your results section



Assumptions


Both variables are continuous (interval/ratio)
The relationship is linear (check with a scatter plot — non-linear relationships yield misleading r values)
Approximately bivariate normal — no extreme outliers (outliers can inflate or deflate r dramatically)


If assumptions are violated, use Spearman’s ρ (rho) — a rank-based non-parametric alternative available in JASP.


How to do it in JASP
Go to Regression → Correlation.






NoteJASP Video Library — Correlation



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#correlation:

▶ Main Analysis — Pearson/Spearman correlation matrix
▶ Plots — scatter plots and correlation matrix visualisation
▶ Partial Correlation — controlling for a third variable

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Simple regression






TipResearch vignette — does advertising spend predict album sales?



A music label tracked advertising budget (€) and first-week sales for a sample of album releases. A simple linear regression of sales on advertising estimates two numbers — the predicted sales of an album that gets no advertising (the intercept b₀), and how much each extra Euro buys you in expected sales (the slope b₁). The same dataset comes back later on this page in the APA reporting example. From DSUJ ch. 8.



What is it?
Simple (linear) regression models the relationship between one predictor (X) and a continuous outcome (Y):
\[Y = b_0 + b_1 X + \varepsilon\]

b₀: the intercept — the predicted value of Y when X = 0
b₁: the slope — the expected change in Y for a one-unit increase in X
ε: the residual error

The R² statistic tells you the proportion of variance in Y explained by the model. Adjusted R² penalises for the number of predictors and is used for model comparison.






TipWhy the intercept (b₀) belongs in the model



You might be tempted to drop the intercept when it isn’t substantively meaningful (e.g. when X = 0 makes no sense). Don’t — omitting it forces the regression line through the origin, which introduces systematic over- and under-estimation. The reported R² can then look misleadingly high because it’s compared against a degenerate null model that predicts zero rather than the mean.
→ Worked example with album sales




Assumptions


Linearity: the relationship between X and Y is linear — check with a scatter plot
Independence of residuals: each observation is independent — check with the Durbin-Watson statistic
Homoscedasticity: the spread of residuals is constant across fitted values — check the residuals vs. fitted plot
Normality of residuals: residuals should be approximately normally distributed — check the residuals Q-Q plot
No influential outliers: cases with high Cook’s distance (> 1 as a rough rule) warrant investigation








TipDiagnosing homoscedasticity at a glance





Plot the standardised residuals against the standardised predictions. A “healthy” cloud of points means error is constant across the range of Y. A funnel shape or a tilted line means the model is more accurate for some predicted values than others — a sign of mis-specification.
→ Visual catalogue: healthy vs. heteroscedastic plots





How to do it in JASP
Go to Regression → Linear Regression.






NoteJASP Video Library — Simple Regression



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression:

▶ Single Predictor — simple regression setup and output
▶ Assumption Checks — residual plots, Q-Q plots
▶ Cook’s Distance — identifying influential cases

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Multiple regression






TipResearch vignette — does narcissism predict Facebook status frequency over and above extraversion?



Ong et al. (2011) measured adolescents’ status-update frequency, demographics (age, grade, sex), extraversion, and narcissism. Asking whether narcissism correlates with status posts isn’t quite the question — extraversion correlates too, and the two traits overlap. A hierarchical regression enters demographics first (Model 0), then extraversion (Model 1), then narcissism (Model 2); the ΔR² at each step is what each block adds over and above what’s already in the model. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 8).
→ Worked in Exercise 3 below.



What is it?
Multiple regression extends simple regression to two or more predictors:
\[Y = b_0 + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots + b_k X_k + \varepsilon\]
Each bⱼ is a partial regression coefficient — it represents the unique effect of predictor Xⱼ on Y, controlling for all other predictors in the model. This is a crucial distinction from simple regression or from looking at correlations in isolation.


Building your model
There are several approaches to entering predictors:







Method
When to use




Simultaneous (Enter)
All predictors entered at once. Use when you have theory-driven reasons to include all predictors


Hierarchical
Predictors entered in blocks based on theoretical priority. Change in R² (ΔR²) for each block tests the incremental contribution


Stepwise
Automated selection based on statistical criteria — generally discouraged for confirmatory research but can be useful exploratory









TipHierarchical regression in JASP





How to set up Model 0 → Model 1 → Model 2 in JASP’s Regression module, what the ΔR² between consecutive models actually means, and why “controlling for X” and “adding X to the null model” are the same operation.





Additional assumption: multicollinearity
When predictors are highly correlated with each other, regression coefficients become unstable. Check: - Tolerance (should be > .1) or VIF (variance inflation factor, should be < 10) — both available in JASP under Collinearity Diagnostics






TipVIF, tolerance, and why 10 isn’t a hard cutoff





What multicollinearity really means (predictors that overlap), how to read VIF (the 10 threshold is a continuum, not a binary pass/fail), and why a scatter plot matrix of your predictors is still worth its weight — VIF only catches linear dependence.





How to do it in JASP






NoteJASP Video Library — Multiple Regression



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression:

▶ Multiple Predictors — multiple regression setup and output
▶ Hierarchical Regression — entering predictors in blocks, ΔR²
▶ Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — robust inference for the coefficients

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Interpreting the regression output
The key tables in JASP output:
Model Summary







Column
What it means




R
Multiple correlation coefficient


R²
Proportion of variance in Y explained by all predictors combined


Adjusted R²
R² corrected for the number of predictors — use this for model comparison


RMSE
Root mean square error — average prediction error in the original units



Coefficients table







Column
What it means




b (Unstandardised)
The slope in original units — “one unit increase in X is associated with a b change in Y, holding all other predictors constant”


β (Standardised)
The slope in standard deviation units — comparable across predictors with different scales


t / p
Whether the slope is significantly different from zero


95% CI
Confidence interval for the slope









TipAPA reporting example



Album sales were significantly predicted by advertising budget, b = 0.096, 95% CI [0.076, 0.115], β = .578, t(196) = 9.98, p < .001. The model explained 33% of the variance in album sales, F(1, 198) = 99.6, p < .001, adjusted R² = .33.

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Dummy coding categorical predictors
Continuous predictors enter regression directly. Categorical predictors must be dummy coded: create k − 1 binary variables (0/1), where k is the number of categories. JASP does this automatically when you define a variable as nominal.
Example: a variable with 3 groups (control, therapy A, therapy B) → 2 dummy variables. The reference category (control) becomes the baseline against which the others are compared.






TipHow predictions and group means come out of the dummies



The intercept becomes the mean of the reference group; each dummy’s coefficient is the difference from that group. Add a covariate (or another categorical predictor) and the coefficients shift slightly — the dummies no longer recover the group means exactly, but the prediction formula still works the same way. With an interaction term added, you do recover each cell mean — at the cost of more parameters.
→ Part 1: one categorical predictor + optional covariate · Part 2: two predictors and the interaction term

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Basics
Non-parametric tests are rank-based alternatives to t-tests and ANOVA. They don’t assume the data follow a particular distribution — making them the go-to choice when the normality assumption is seriously violated (often in small samples, or with strong outliers or floor/ceiling effects).
This page covers the four most commonly used non-parametric tests for group comparison, each one a counterpart to a parametric procedure you already know:

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
What do they actually do?
All four tests follow the same idea: convert your raw scores to ranks, then test whether the ranks differ between groups (or conditions). Because ranks are bounded and don’t depend on the original metric, the resulting test statistic isn’t pulled around by outliers or skew the way a t or F statistic can be.






TipWhy “rank” the data?





Imagine one participant in your dataset reports a reaction time of 12,000 ms while everyone else is between 200–800 ms. That single value drags the mean of their group up and inflates the variance of the difference — your t drops, your p climbs, and you may miss a real effect. Replace each raw score by its rank (1 = fastest, N = slowest), and that outlier becomes “the slowest one” — its influence is bounded. You trade away some information (the absolute spacing of the values) for robustness against extreme scores.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
section: When to use a non-parametric test
text:
When to use a non-parametric test

The normality assumption of the parametric test is seriously violated, especially in small samples (n < ~30) where you can’t rely on the central limit theorem
You have ordinal data (e.g. Likert items treated strictly as ranks), where means aren’t really meaningful
You have strong outliers that you don’t want to remove and that distort the parametric test







WarningNon-parametric ≠ assumption-free



The Mann–Whitney test does not test for equal medians unless the two distributions have the same shape. If group A is symmetric and group B is heavily skewed, a significant Mann–Whitney result could reflect that difference in shape — not a median shift. Always inspect the distributions before interpreting.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
section: How to do them in JASP
text:
How to do them in JASP
In JASP, every non-parametric test lives alongside its parametric counterpart:

Wilcoxon rank-sum / signed-rank → T-Tests menu → tick the Wilcoxon option
Kruskal–Wallis → ANOVA menu → tick the Kruskal-Wallis test option (under Nonparametrics)
Friedman → Repeated Measures ANOVA menu → tick the Friedman option

JASP reports the rank-based statistic, a p-value, and a rank-biserial correlation as the effect size for the Wilcoxon tests.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Interpreting the output








Test
Statistic
Effect size




Wilcoxon rank-sum
W (or U)
rank-biserial correlation


Wilcoxon signed-rank
V
rank-biserial correlation (matched-rank)


Kruskal–Wallis
H (≈ χ²)
ε² or η²H


Friedman
χ²F
Kendall’s W



Report the test statistic, p-value, and an effect size. Where relevant, also report group medians (and IQRs) rather than means.






TipAPA reporting example



A Wilcoxon rank-sum test showed that reaction times were significantly higher in the alcohol condition (Mdn = 540, IQR = 180) than in the control condition (Mdn = 420, IQR = 140), W = 312, p = .008, rank-biserial r = .42.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Post-hoc tests
A significant Kruskal–Wallis or Friedman test tells you that at least two groups (conditions) differ — but not which. JASP offers Dunn’s post-hoc tests with Bonferroni or Holm correction directly in the same dialog.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
