title: T-Tests
text:
Basics
t-tests are used to compare means — either against a known value, or between two groups. They are one of the most widely used inferential tests in psychology.

title: T-Tests
text:
Three types of t-test








Test
Research question
Example




One-sample
Does my sample mean differ from a known value?
Is the mean IQ of my sample different from the population mean of 100?


Paired-samples
Do two related measurements differ?
Do anxiety scores decrease from pre- to post-therapy within the same participants?


Independent-samples
Do two independent groups differ?
Do males and females differ in their extraversion scores?




Research vignettes — what each test can answer






TipOne-sample t-test — are people any good at guessing their own IQ?



Students report what they think their IQ is. The IQ scale is constructed to have a population mean of 100, so a one-sample t-test against 100 asks whether students systematically over- or under-estimate themselves. A one-sentence test of a popular folk-psychology claim. From SSR Lecture 11; the same setup appears in DSUJ ch. 9.








TipPaired-samples t-test — does festival hygiene drop over the weekend?



The same festival-goers were rated for hygiene on day 1 and day 3 of a UK rock festival. Each participant gives two scores, so a paired-samples t-test on the day-1 − day-3 differences asks whether the typical attendee gets visibly grottier. DSUJ ch. 9 uses this dataset to introduce paired tests, then immediately points out that the differences are skewed enough to want a Wilcoxon as a sanity check.
→ Worked in Exercise 3 below.








TipIndependent-samples t-test — are senior business managers as histrionic as clinical inpatients?



Board & Fritzon (2005) compared personality profiles of senior business managers with a clinical sample of inpatients diagnosed with personality disorders. Each participant belongs to one group, so an independent-samples t-test on the histrionic scale asks whether boardroom and ward differ on a trait often associated with executive charisma. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 9).
→ Worked in Exercise 2 below.

title: T-Tests
text:
When to use which t-test

One-sample: you have a single group and want to compare its mean to a fixed reference value (e.g., population norm, chance level, zero)
Paired-samples (also called dependent-samples or repeated-measures t-test): the same participants measured twice, or participants matched in pairs — the two measurements are not independent
Independent-samples: two separate, unrelated groups — each participant contributes one score







WarningDon’t confuse paired and independent



Using an independent-samples t-test when you have a repeated-measures design throws away half the information and inflates your error term. Always identify whether measurements within rows of your dataset are linked.








TipThe paired t-test is just a one-sample t-test on difference scores





For each participant, compute the difference between their two measurements. Now you have one set of difference scores — and you’re back to a one-sample t-test against H₀: mean difference = 0. This is why “paired”, “dependent-samples”, and “within-subjects” all refer to the same procedure.

title: T-Tests
text:
One-sided or two-sided?
Whichever t-test you pick, you’ll also need to decide what alternative hypothesis to test:

Two-sided (default in JASP): H₁ says the mean differs from the null value in either direction. Use this unless you have a strong directional prediction.
One-sided (directional): H₁ says the mean is specifically greater than, or specifically less than, the null value. More statistical power if you’re right about the direction — but if the effect goes the other way, you can’t reject H₀.







TipA one-sided test is a commitment, not a shortcut



Commit to a one-sided test before looking at your data — ideally pre-registered. Switching to one-sided after seeing a result in your favoured direction is a common form of p-hacking.
→ Visual explainer: how the rejection region changes between one-sided and two-sided








TipChoosing your alternative hypothesis





Using the IQ self-assessment example: two-sided (“self-reported IQ is different from 120”) asks whether there’s any bias, while one-sided (“students systematically overestimate IQ”) tests a specific direction. Different research questions, different rejection regions.

title: T-Tests
text:
Assumptions


Scale data: the dependent variable is continuous (interval or ratio level)
Independence of observations: each row in the dataset is an independent participant (violated in paired designs, which is why you use the paired t-test instead)
Normality: the dependent variable (or, for paired data, the difference scores) should be approximately normally distributed, especially important in small samples (n < 30)
Equal variances (independent-samples only): Levene’s test checks this. JASP reports a Welch correction by default, which is robust to unequal variances — this is generally the safer choice








TipWhy “always use Welch” is the safer default





A subtle point: significance tests for assumptions (Levene’s, Shapiro-Wilk) are themselves driven by sample size. At large n, even a trivial deviation from equal variances will make Levene’s significant — that doesn’t mean you have a “problem”. At small n, neither test has the power to detect a meaningful violation. The recommended workflow: look at the observed standard deviations (a ratio of more than ~1.4 between groups is worth noting), inspect Q-Q plots, and just default to the Welch t-test — it costs you a little power and buys robustness against unequal variances.




Checking normality
Use the Q-Q plot and inspect skewness/kurtosis in Descriptives (see the Descriptives page). The Shapiro-Wilk test is available in JASP under the assumption checks, but is sensitive to sample size — a visual check is often more informative.

title: T-Tests
text:
Effect size
Always report an effect size alongside your p-value. For t-tests, use Cohen’s d:



d
Conventional label




0.2
Small


0.5
Medium


0.8
Large



These benchmarks are rough guides — the meaningful effect size depends on your research context. Tick Cohen’s d under Effect Size in the JASP options panel.

title: T-Tests
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Go to T-Tests in the analysis menu and select the appropriate test.






NoteJASP Video Library — T-Tests



All screen recordings are at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_T-Tests.html:
Independent-samples t-test

▶ Main Analysis — setting up the test, grouping variable
▶ Assumption Checks — normality and homogeneity of variance
▶ Effect Size — requesting Cohen’s d and confidence intervals
▶ Plots — raincloud or bar plots for group differences

One-sample and paired-samples t-test

▶ One-Sample — comparing a mean to a fixed reference value
▶ Paired-Samples — pre/post measurements or matched pairs

title: T-Tests
text:
Interpreting the output
The JASP output for an independent-samples t-test contains:

t: the test statistic — the ratio of the mean difference to the standard error of the difference
df: degrees of freedom (for Welch’s test, this will not be a whole number)
p: the two-tailed p-value. Reject H₀ (no difference) if p < your α level (typically .05)
Mean difference: the raw difference between group means — report this alongside the SE or CI
Cohen’s d: the effect size







TipAPA reporting example



An independent-samples t-test showed that the anxiety ratings of participants shown a picture of a spider (M = 47.0, SD = 9.3) were significantly higher than those of participants shown a picture of a real spider (M = 40.0, SD = 9.3), t(21.4) = 1.68, p = .108, d = 0.71. (This is an example — adjust numbers and interpretation to your own results.)

text:
Non-parametric alternative
If your data strongly violate the normality assumption (and your sample is small), use the Wilcoxon tests instead:



t-test
Non-parametric equivalent




One-sample
Wilcoxon signed-rank test


Paired-samples
Wilcoxon signed-rank test


Independent-samples
Mann–Whitney U test (Wilcoxon rank-sum test)



These are available under T-Tests → Nonparametric in JASP.

title: ANOVA
text:
Basics
Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) extends the logic of the t-test to situations with three or more groups, multiple factors, covariates, or repeated measurements. It answers the question: are there significant differences in means across conditions?
This page covers four ANOVA designs. Jump to the relevant section:

title: ANOVA
text:
One-way ANOVA






TipResearch vignette — do the four Hogwarts houses differ in Machiavellianism?



Jakob et al. (2019) measured Machiavellianism in students who identified as Gryffindor, Hufflepuff, Ravenclaw, or Slytherin: four independent groups, one continuous personality scale. A one-way ANOVA asks whether any house differs from the others; a planned contrast (Slytherin vs the rest) tests the obvious follow-up. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 11).
→ Worked in Exercise 1 below.



What is it?
One-way ANOVA tests whether the means of three or more independent groups differ on a continuous dependent variable. The “one-way” refers to a single grouping factor (independent variable).
The F-statistic is the ratio of between-groups variance to within-groups variance. A large F indicates that the group means are more spread out than expected by chance.






TipWhere the F-statistic comes from





F is the ratio of the mean squares of the model (how much variance the group means capture) to the mean squares of the error (leftover within-group variance). Like a t-statistic, F is a standardised test statistic with a known sampling distribution — that’s how a p-value gets attached to it.





Assumptions


Independence: each participant contributes data to only one group
Normality: the DV should be approximately normally distributed within each group (check with Q-Q plot of residuals)
Homogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity): the variance should be similar across groups — check with Levene’s test or observed group variances. If violated, use Welch’s ANOVA (available in JASP)








TipWhy assumptions actually matter (small demonstration)





When the DV is heavy-tailed (e.g. reaction times with extreme values), the empirical sampling distribution of F under H₀ no longer matches the theoretical F-distribution. The result: your α and power aren’t what you think they are. ANOVA is relatively robust to mild non-normality, but vulnerable to extreme observations that inflate within-group variance.
→ Simulation: what 100,000 ANOVAs under H₀ actually look like





Effect size
Report η² (eta squared) or the less biased ω² (omega squared). For factorial designs, make sure to use the partial versions of these effect sizes (partial η² & partial ω²).



η²
Conventional label




.01
Small


.06
Medium


.14
Large









TipEffect size vs. test statistic — what’s the difference?





A t (or F) statistic grows with both the magnitude of the effect and the sample size — increase n with a fixed mean difference and t climbs accordingly. Effect sizes like Cohen’s d, r, or η² are standardised and largely insulated from sample size. That’s why a significant result with a tiny effect size can still be practically meaningless, and why both belong in your report.
→ Worked example with numbers





Post-hoc tests
A significant F-test only tells you that at least one pair of groups differs — not which pairs. Request post-hoc tests (e.g., Tukey HSD) or planned contrasts to identify specific differences. JASP provides both.


How to do it in JASP
Go to ANOVA → ANOVA in the analysis menu.






NoteJASP Video Library — One-Way ANOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-oneway:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up one-way ANOVA, post-hoc tests
▶ Normality and Homogeneity Check — assumption checks
▶ Effect Size — requesting η² and ω²
▶ Descriptive Plots — visualising group means

title: ANOVA
text:
ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)






TipResearch vignette — does interpretation training leave children with a negative bias?



Muris et al. (2008) randomly assigned children to one of three training conditions (negative / positive / no information about an ambiguous scenario), then measured an interpretation-bias score later. Age, gender, and natural anxiety vary between kids and would muddy a plain one-way ANOVA, so an ANCOVA pulls those covariates out and lets the training comparison stand on equal footing. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 12).
→ Worked in Exercise 2 below.



What is it?
ANCOVA extends ANOVA by adding one or more continuous covariates — variables you want to statistically control for. By removing the variance explained by the covariate, ANCOVA gives a more sensitive test of the group effect.
Example: comparing therapy outcomes across three groups while accounting for pre-therapy symptom severity.


Key additional assumption

Homogeneity of regression slopes: the relationship between the covariate and the DV should be the same in all groups. JASP can test this by adding a Group × Covariate interaction — it should not be significant. If the interaction effect is significant, you need to take this into account when interpreting the main effect of group. For instance, this therapy is effective for people in group A, if they have low pre-therapy symptom severity. (Unfamiliar with what an “interaction” is? See the callout in the Factorial ANOVA section below.)







TipHow ANCOVA partitions variance (with worked example)





Once you add a covariate, the regression coefficients for the group dummies no longer recover the raw group means — they recover means adjusted for the covariate. The F-tests for group and covariate then come from comparing the full model against reduced models that drop one predictor at a time.
→ Worked example: extraversion ~ nationality + openness





How to do it in JASP
Add covariates in the Covariates box when setting up an ANOVA.






NoteJASP Video Library — ANCOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#ancova:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up ANCOVA, adding the covariate
▶ Add Interaction — testing homogeneity of regression slopes
▶ Descriptive Plots — marginal means after controlling for the covariate

title: ANOVA
text:
Factorial ANOVA






TipResearch vignette — does mood × “stop rule” affect compulsive checking?



Davey et al. (2003) crossed two factors — induced mood (negative vs positive) × stop-rule for an ambiguous task (“as many as can” / “feel like continuing” / no instruction) — and measured how much each participant checked. The substantive question is the interaction: does the effect of mood depend on which stop-rule you got? The two main effects on their own can be misleading when the interaction is doing the work. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 13).
→ Worked in Exercise 3 below.



What is it?
Factorial ANOVA includes two or more between-subjects factors. In addition to the main effect of each factor, it tests the interaction — whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of another.
Example: testing whether a new intervention is effective (factor 1: intervention vs. control) and whether this depends on participant gender (factor 2: male/female/other).


Interpreting interactions
An interaction means the effect of factor A is different at different levels of factor B. Always examine interaction plots — JASP provides these under Descriptive plots. A significant interaction means you should interpret simple main effects rather than main effects alone.






TipWhat “interaction” really means





A concrete example: does the effect of alcohol on driving ability depend on the dose of speed (methamphetamine) the driver has also taken? Alcohol impairs driving on its own, and so does speed — the interaction term asks whether the two together do more (or less) damage than you’d guess from adding their separate effects. If somebody who’s taken a high dose of speed shows a bigger alcohol-related impairment than someone who hasn’t, that’s the interaction in action.





How to do it in JASP






NoteJASP Video Library — Factorial ANOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-factorial:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up a two-way ANOVA
▶ Plots — interaction plots
▶ Simple Main Effects — follow-up after a significant interaction
▶ Post-Hoc Tests — pairwise comparisons

title: ANOVA
text:
Repeated-measures ANOVA






TipResearch vignette — are splattered cadavers distracting during serial recall?



Each participant did a serial-recall task three times — once with no music, once with classical, once with death metal with explicit lyrics — so the music factor is within-subjects. A repeated-measures ANOVA uses the within-person correlation to ask whether genre actually shifts memory performance, with Mauchly’s test and a Greenhouse–Geisser correction handling the sphericity assumption that comes in once you have three or more levels. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 14).
→ Worked in Exercise 4 below.



What is it?
Repeated-measures ANOVA is used when the same participants are measured across multiple conditions or time points (within-subjects design). Because measurements within a person are correlated, this design is more powerful than a between-subjects design of the same size.
Mixed designs contain both a within-subjects factor (e.g., time: pre, post, follow-up) and a between-subjects factor (e.g., treatment group).


Key additional assumption: sphericity
For within-subjects factors with three or more levels, ANOVA assumes sphericity — that the variances of the differences between all pairs of levels are equal. JASP performs Mauchly’s test automatically:

If sphericity is violated (p < .05): use the Greenhouse-Geisser or Huynh-Feldt correction (JASP offers both)
If there are only two within-subjects levels, sphericity cannot be violated (it is automatic)







TipSphericity, Mauchly, and “just always apply the correction”





Sphericity = the variances of the differences between every pair of within-subject levels are equal. Mauchly’s test flags violations, but (like Levene’s and Shapiro-Wilk) it’s sensitive to sample size. The pragmatic move: under Assumption Checks → Sphericity corrections, tick Greenhouse-Geisser (or Huynh-Feldt) by default — they adjust the degrees of freedom in the ANOVA table to give you a result that’s robust to sphericity violations whether or not Mauchly is significant.





How to do it in JASP
Go to ANOVA → Repeated Measures ANOVA.






NoteJASP Video Library — Repeated-Measures ANOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-repeated:

▶ Main Analysis — defining within-subjects factors and levels
▶ Sphericity Test — Mauchly’s test and Greenhouse-Geisser correction
▶ Plots — profile plots for within-subjects designs
▶ Contrasts — planned comparisons
▶ Post-Hoc Tests — pairwise comparisons with correction

title: ANOVA
text:
Interpreting the ANOVA output
The ANOVA table shows:







Column
What it means




F
Test statistic — variance between groups / variance within groups


df
Degrees of freedom — (number of groups − 1) for the effect, (N − number of groups) for the error


p
Significance — reject H₀ if p < α


η² or ω²
Effect size









TipAPA reporting



F(df_effect, df_error) = [value], p = [value], η² = [value].
Example: F(2, 57) = 4.32, p = .018, η² = .13.
For repeated measures with a correction: F(1.76, 46.5) = 5.21, p = .011, η² = .17 (Greenhouse-Geisser corrected).

title: ANOVA
text:
Non-parametric alternatives
If ANOVA assumptions are seriously violated:



ANOVA design
Non-parametric alternative




One-way (between)
Kruskal–Wallis test


One-way (within)
Friedman test



Both are available under ANOVA → Nonparametric in JASP. See the Non-Parametric Tests page for the full walkthrough.

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Basics
Before running any inferential test, look at your data. Descriptives and plots tell you the shape of the distribution, flag outliers, and reveal whether the assumptions of your next analysis are even close to being met. Surprising patterns often only show up here.

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
What are descriptive statistics?
Descriptive statistics summarise the key features of a dataset without making inferences about a population. The most important are:
Central tendency — where is the middle of the distribution?



Statistic
Use when




Mean
Continuous data, approximately symmetric distribution


Median
Skewed distributions or ordinal data


Mode
Categorical data, or to describe the most common value



Spread — how much do values vary?







Statistic
What it tells you




Standard deviation (SD)
Average distance from the mean (same units as the data)


Variance
SD² — less intuitive but used in many formulas


Range
Max − Min — sensitive to outliers


Interquartile range (IQR)
Middle 50% of values — robust to outliers



Shape — is the distribution symmetric?







Statistic
What it tells you




Skewness
Whether the tail is longer on the left (negative) or right (positive)


Kurtosis
How heavy or light the tails are relative to a normal distribution

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
When to use descriptive statistics
Always — as the first step in every analysis. Specifically:

Before any test: report means and SDs for your key variables
To check normality: look at histograms and Q-Q plots, and inspect skewness/kurtosis values
To detect outliers: boxplots flag values beyond 1.5 × IQR from the quartiles
To describe your sample: demographics (age, gender, group sizes) always require descriptives

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Plots — visualising your data
The descriptives table summarises the data; a plot shows it. Which plot to use depends on the measurement level of the variable and whether you’re looking at one distribution or comparing several.

One continuous variable








Plot
What it shows
When to prefer it




Histogram
Shape: skew, modality, gaps, ceiling or floor
Always your first look — the eye catches things skewness numbers miss


Boxplot
Median, IQR, candidate outliers past the whiskers
When the centre and outliers are what you care about


Q-Q plot
Sample quantiles vs the theoretical normal quantiles
For the normality assumption — straighter line, closer to normal


Density plot
A smoothed histogram
When n is small and the histogram looks blocky









TipHistogram + boxplot is the workhorse pair





The histogram shows the shape; the boxplot pins down where the action is (median, quartiles, tails). The festival-hygiene example in DSUJ ch. 5 is a nice illustration: the day-1 boxplot flags one extreme outlier off the top of the scale, and the histogram then explains why the mean and median don’t agree.





Comparing continuous distributions across groups








Plot
What it shows
When to prefer it




Raincloud plot
Half-violin density + boxplot + raw points, one strip per group
The first plot to reach for. Defaults to this.


Boxplots side-by-side
Medians and IQRs together
When you have many groups and the raincloud gets crowded


Bar plot with error bars
Means + SE or CI per group
APA still expects these in papers, but pair with a raincloud while exploring


Scatter with group colour
Two continuous variables, group as colour or shape
When the group comparison sits inside a continuous relationship









WarningBar-and-error-bar plots hide a lot



Two groups with identical means and SEs can have very different underlying shapes (one bimodal, one symmetric, one carried by a single extreme value). On a bar plot they look the same. SSR Lecture 4 and DSUJ ch. 5 both call this out; rainclouds caught on in psychology as the practical fix.




Two continuous variables

Scatter plot — the honest summary of a relationship. Look at it before computing a correlation, not after; non-linearity and extreme points are usually obvious.
Scatter-plot matrix — with several continuous variables, JASP’s Plot Matrix in Descriptives gives you every pairwise scatter at once. Useful as a first sweep.



Categorical data








Plot
What it shows
When to prefer it




Bar plot of counts
Frequency of each category
The default for a single nominal or ordinal variable


Pie chart
Proportions of a whole
Rarely the best choice — humans read angles poorly; a bar plot is usually clearer


Stacked / grouped bars
One categorical variable split by another
The “is X related to Y” picture before a chi-square test


Mosaic plot
Cell area proportional to frequency
Same data as a stacked bar, but the joint distribution is more visible



In JASP these all live under Descriptives → Plots, or Frequencies → Distribution Plots for the categorical side.






TipPlot first, test after





Anscombe’s quartet — four datasets with identical means, SDs, correlations, and regression lines, but visibly different shapes — is the classic warning that summary statistics can mislead. SSR Lecture 4 walks through it; DSUJ ch. 4 covers the same idea with real psychology data. A p-value never replaces a look at the data.

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Assumptions to check
Descriptive statistics have no assumptions themselves, but examining them tells you whether the assumptions of your next analysis hold:


Normality: histograms should be roughly bell-shaped; skewness and kurtosis should be close to 0 (rough rule: |value| < 1 for reasonable samples, though this depends on sample size)
Outliers: inspect boxplots — individual points beyond the whiskers warrant investigation
Floor/ceiling effects: if most values cluster at the minimum or maximum, your scale may not have sufficient range

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Open your dataset, go to Descriptives → Descriptives in the analysis menu.






NoteJASP Video Library — Descriptives



All screen recordings are at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Descriptives.html:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up descriptives, requesting mean, SD, and frequency tables
▶ Split Variable — computing descriptives separately per group
▶ Raincloud Plots — raw data + density + boxplot in one figure
▶ Distribution and Boxplots — exploring spread and outliers
▶ Scatter Plot — visualising relationships between two continuous variables
▶ Assumption Checks — Q-Q plots and normality tests

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Interpreting the output
After running Descriptives in JASP, the output table shows your requested statistics. Key things to check:

N: the number of valid (non-missing) observations per variable
Mean ± SD: the central tendency and spread — always report these together
Skewness / Kurtosis: both should be close to 0 for a normal distribution. Divide each value by its standard error to get a z-score; |z| > 3.29 flags a significant deviation (in large samples, use visual inspection instead)
Boxplot whiskers: extend to 1.5 × IQR; individual points beyond the whiskers are potential outliers







TipReporting in APA format



For continuous variables: M = [mean], SD = [SD], N = [n]. For frequency data: report counts and percentages.

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Further reading

DSUJ Chapter 5 — Exploring data with graphs — covers histograms, boxplots, violin plots, and scatter plots in detail with JASP walkthroughs
DSUJ Chapter 6 — Assumptions — normality tests, outlier detection, and robust alternatives

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Basics
When both your variables are categorical, the appropriate tools come from the chi-square family. A contingency table (cross-tabulation) shows the frequency of every combination of categories, and a chi-square test asks whether the two variables are independent.
This page covers:

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Chi-square test of independence






TipResearch vignette — do sexualised images of female athletes shape women’s self-evaluations?



Daniels (2012) randomly assigned women to see either sexualised or performance-focused images of female athletes, then coded each participant’s open-ended response for whether it expressed self-objectification (yes/no). Two categorical variables — image type (2 levels) × self-objectification (2 levels) — make a 2 × 2 contingency table; a chi-square test of independence then asks whether the image manipulation pushed women into a different way of talking about themselves. From Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 19).
→ Worked in Exercise 1 below.



What is it?
The chi-square test of independence asks: are two categorical variables related, or independent? It compares the observed cell frequencies in a contingency table against the expected frequencies under the null hypothesis of independence.
\[\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}\]
Example: do voting preferences (Party A / Party B / Party C) differ across age groups (18–30, 31–50, 51+)? The contingency table is 3 × 3; the null hypothesis is that age group and party preference are independent.


When to use it

Both variables are categorical (nominal or ordinal treated as nominal)
Each participant contributes to exactly one cell of the table (one row per participant)
You want to test whether membership in one variable’s categories is related to membership in the other’s



Assumptions


Independence: each observation is independent of the others (no participant appears in more than one cell)
Expected cell counts: all expected counts should be ≥ 5 (a common rule of thumb); at minimum, no more than 20% of cells should have expected counts below 5
If expected counts are too small, use Fisher’s exact test instead (see below)




Effect size
Report Cramér’s V (or φ for a 2 × 2 table):



Cramér’s V
Conventional label (df = 1)




.10
Small


.30
Medium


.50
Large



For larger tables, the benchmarks scale down — consult DSUJ Chapter 19 or use JASP’s effect size output.


How to do it in JASP
Go to Frequencies → Contingency Tables in the analysis menu.






NoteJASP Video Library — Contingency Tables



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Frequencies.html:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up the table, requesting chi-square
▶ Cells — observed counts, expected counts, residuals
▶ Statistics — Cramér’s V and other effect sizes





Interpreting the output







Statistic
What it means




χ²
The test statistic — bigger means observed frequencies deviate more from expected


df
(rows − 1) × (columns − 1)


p
Reject H₀ (independence) if p < α


Cramér’s V
Effect size — the strength of association



If the test is significant, inspect the standardised residuals to see which cells contribute most to the deviation from independence.






TipAPA reporting example



A chi-square test of independence showed a significant association between age group and party preference, χ²(4) = 18.6, p < .001, Cramér’s V = .24. Inspection of the standardised residuals indicated that voters aged 51+ were over-represented among Party A supporters.

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Chi-square goodness-of-fit
A variant of the chi-square test for one categorical variable against a hypothesised distribution. Example: does a die roll uniformly (each face expected at 1/6) over 600 throws?
In JASP: Frequencies → Multinomial Test. Specify either equal expected probabilities or a custom expected distribution.

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Fisher’s exact test






TipResearch vignette — Fisher’s “lady tasting tea”



The historical origin of the test, from R. A. Fisher’s The Design of Experiments (1935): a lady (Muriel Bristol) claimed she could tell whether the milk had been poured into the cup before or after the tea. Fisher prepared 8 cups, 4 each way, in random order. Expected cell counts well below 5 make the standard chi-square approximation unreliable, so Fisher derived the exact test by enumerating every cup-arrangement with the same row and column totals. The same logic applies any time you have a 2 × 2 table from a small sample.


When expected cell counts are too small for the chi-square approximation to be trustworthy (typically a 2 × 2 table with any expected count < 5), Fisher’s exact test computes the exact probability of the observed table under the null. It is available in the same JASP dialog — just tick Fisher’s exact test under Statistics.






TipWhy Fisher’s exact, not chi-square, with small expected counts





The chi-square statistic only approximately follows the χ² distribution; the approximation breaks down when expected counts are small. Fisher’s exact test side-steps the approximation altogether by enumerating every possible table with the same row and column totals. It costs nothing in interpretation — you still report a p-value — and is the safer choice for sparse tables.

Using leni_16a.jasp, run a chi-square test of independence on image type × self-objectification. Inspect the standardised residuals to see which cells drive the result.

title: Power Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
A power analysis answers the question: given my study design, what sample size will I need to detect the effect I care about? Done before data collection, it is the standard tool for sample-size justification in (pre-)registered studies and a core component of transparent statistical practice (Lakens, 2022).

title: Power Analysis
section: Type I and Type II errors
text:
Type I and Type II errors
A statistical test can be wrong in two ways (DSUJ §2.10.7; Agresti, Franklin & Klingenberg §9.4):









Reality: H₀ true (no effect)
Reality: H₁ true (effect exists)




Decision: don’t reject H₀
Correct
Type II error (false negative)


Decision: reject H₀
Type I error (false positive)
Correct




α = probability of a Type I error. By convention .05.
β = probability of a Type II error. Cohen (1988, 1992) suggested .20 as the maximum acceptable value, and this has become the conventional minimum — meaning we are willing to miss a real effect at most once every five replications.
Power = 1 − β = the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis. Conventional minimum: .80 (= 80%).







TipThe trade-off between α and β





For a fixed sample size, making α smaller (e.g., .01 instead of .05) raises the bar for “evidence” and so reduces Type I error — but increases Type II error, because real effects also produce smaller test statistics. To shrink both α and β simultaneously, you need a larger sample size. Agresti, Franklin & Klingenberg (§9.4) describe this as “the two probabilities are inversely related” for a given N. Power analysis is the formal calculation of how large N must be to keep both errors acceptably small (Cohen, 1988).

title: Power Analysis
text:
The four quantities
Power, α, sample size, and effect size are mathematically linked: given any three, the fourth is determined. Most planning questions fall into one of three modes:









Mode
What you fix
What you solve for
When to use




A priori
Effect size, α, power
Sample size N
Planning a new study


Sensitivity
N, α, power
Minimum detectable effect size
When N is constrained (clinical trial with limited recruitment); answers “what is the smallest effect I could realistically catch?”


Post hoc / achieved
N, effect size, α
Power
Reporting realised power for a published study



A priori power analysis is the dominant mode and the only one suitable for sample-size justification in pre-registration. Post-hoc power calculated from the observed effect size in the same data has well-known mathematical problems and should not be reported (Lakens, 2022).

title: Power Analysis
section: The central question: what effect size?
text:
The central question: what effect size?
Effect size is the input that drives sample-size estimation. The temptation is to use a published estimate from a similar study; the problem is that published effect sizes are systematically inflated by publication bias and other selection processes. Lakens (2022) argues for a different framing.

Smallest effect size of interest (SESOI)
The most defensible approach is to plan for the smallest effect size that would be theoretically or practically meaningful to detect. This is what the JASP Power module asks for in its UI — the input is literally labelled “Minimal effect size of interest” (δ). Picking the SESOI requires you to commit, before data collection, to what would count as a non-trivial effect — exactly the substantive question you should be answering anyway.
In practice, SESOI is justified by:

Theoretical considerations — what is the minimum effect that the theory would predict, or the minimum effect that would change theory if observed?
Practical / clinical relevance — what is the smallest effect that would matter for the intervention, the decision, or the patient?
Resource constraints — what is the smallest effect that your available N could realistically detect, used in a sensitivity analysis to declare what you can and cannot say?
Cohen’s benchmarks — small / medium / large conventions per analysis type. Useful as a fallback when no substantive anchor is available, but explicitly second-best.




Analysis
Effect-size metric
Cohen’s “small”
“medium”
“large”




t-test
Cohen’s d
0.20
0.50
0.80


Correlation
r
.10
.30
.50


ANOVA
Cohen’s f
0.10
0.25
0.40


Multiple regression
f²
0.02
0.15
0.35


Chi-square
w
0.10
0.30
0.50









WarningDon’t reverse-engineer the effect size to fit your sample



Picking the effect size that exactly fits your available N at .80 power tells you nothing other than that you’ve found a tautology. Always commit to the SESOI on substantive grounds first, then solve for N. If your available N can’t support that SESOI, run a sensitivity analysis and be honest about the limits in the discussion.

title: Power Analysis
section: How to do it in JASP — the Power module
text:
How to do it in JASP — the Power module
JASP’s Power module (jaspPower, originally based on Richard Morey’s jpower) is installed by clicking the blue + icon in the top-right of the JASP window to open the module list, then enabling Power. Once enabled, it appears as a top-level analysis menu.
The module is built around the pwr R package and handles closed-form power calculations for the following tests:







Test
What it computes




Independent-samples t-test
Two-group comparison; supports unequal sample-size ratio (N₁/N₂)


Paired-samples t-test
Within-subjects design


One-sample t-test
Sample mean vs known value


One-sample z-test
When σ is known


One-sample proportion test
Single proportion vs hypothesised value


Two-samples proportion test
Two independent proportions


One-sample variance-ratio test
Sample variance vs reference


Two-samples variance-ratio test
Comparing two variances









WarningANOVA, regression, and chi-square are not (yet) in jaspPower



At the time of writing, the module covers the tests above. ANOVA, multiple regression, and chi-square power analyses are not yet implemented in JASP — see the “Complementary tools” section below.



Workflow

Pick the Statistical test from the top dropdown.
Pick what to calculate: Sample Size N, Power, or Effect size — this sets the mode (a priori, post hoc, or sensitivity).
Enter the other two quantities (e.g., for a priori: effect size + power; α defaults to .05).
Choose the alternative hypothesis (two-sided / one-sided).
For two-group tests, set the sample-size ratio (N₁/N₂; defaults to 1).
Tick the plots you want under Plots:

Power contour plot — the trade-off surface across effect size and N.
Power demonstration — overlaid null and alternative sampling distributions, with the shaded rejection region — useful for teaching.
Power curve by effect size — power as a function of effect size for the chosen N.
Power curve by N — power as a function of N for the chosen effect size.
Explanatory text — JASP writes out a plain-language interpretation of the calculation.

(Optional) Open the Data Generation section to simulate a synthetic dataset matching your power-analysis parameters — useful for piloting an analysis pipeline before real data arrive.

title: Power Analysis
text:
Reporting power analysis






TipWhat a sample-size justification should contain (Lakens, 2022)




The smallest effect size of interest and its justification — why this size and not smaller.
The intended analysis (test, alpha level, alternative hypothesis).
The desired power and the calculation mode (a priori, sensitivity).
The software used (e.g., “JASP 0.18 Power module” or “G*Power 3.1”) with version.
The resulting target N (for a priori) or minimum detectable effect (for sensitivity).
Any deviations from the plan after data collection, with rationale.

Example: “We aimed to detect a between-groups effect of at least d = 0.40 — the smallest effect we considered theoretically meaningful, anchored on a meta-analytic estimate of the literature minus a publication-bias correction. An a priori power analysis with the JASP Power module (α = .05 two-sided, 1 − β = .80, two-sample t-test) returned a required N = 100 per group (200 total). We pre-registered this target and collected to that N.”

title: Power Analysis
text:
Further reading

Lakens, D. (2022). Sample size justification. Collabra: Psychology, 8(1), 33267. — The current best-practice guide. Reframes power analysis around the smallest effect size of interest rather than published effect sizes.
Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical power analysis for the behavioral sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum. — The foundational reference; source of the small/medium/large conventions.
Cohen, J. (1992). A power primer. Psychological Bulletin, 112(1), 155–159. — The concise version of Cohen (1988); still widely cited and still useful.
DSUJ §2.10.7 (Type I and Type II errors). — The conceptual framing used here; reproduces Cohen’s β ≤ .20 convention.
Agresti, Franklin & Klingenberg §9.4 + §9.6 (Decisions, errors, and power). — Accessible textbook treatment of the conceptual foundations.
Faul, F., Erdfelder, E., Lang, A.-G., & Buchner, A. (2007). G*Power 3: A flexible statistical power analysis program for the social, behavioral, and biomedical sciences. Behavior Research Methods, 39(2), 175–191.
Champely, S. (2020). pwr: Basic Functions for Power Analysis. R package — the engine behind jaspPower.

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
Latent Class Analysis (LCA) and Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) are person-centred approaches that identify unobserved subgroups in a population — “classes” or “profiles” of individuals who respond similarly across a set of indicators and differently from people in other subgroups. In contrast to variable-centred methods (factor analysis, regression), which describe how variables relate, LCA and LPA describe how people cluster (Collins & Lanza, 2010, §1.2.3).
Statistically, both are forms of finite mixture modelling: the assumption is that the population is a mixture of k unobserved subgroups, each with its own response distribution, and the data don’t tell you which subgroup each respondent belongs to.

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
The LCA model
Following Collins & Lanza (2010, §2.5), the LCA model has two kinds of parameters:

Latent class prevalences (γ): the proportion of the population belonging to each class. With C classes, C − 1 are estimated (the last one is determined by 1 minus the sum).
Item-response probabilities (ρ): for each item and each response category, the probability of giving that response conditional on being in a particular class. With J items each having Rj categories and C classes, C · Σ(Rj − 1) item-response probabilities are estimated.

The LPA analogue replaces the item-response probabilities with within-class means and variances for each continuous indicator.

Local independence — the defining assumption
LCA assumes that conditional on the latent class, the observed indicators are independent of each other (Collins & Lanza, 2010, §2.5.2). Any correlation between two items is, under the model, entirely explained by their shared dependence on the latent class. If two items remain correlated even within a class, the local-independence assumption is violated and you’ll likely need to either (a) extract more classes, (b) drop an item, or (c) freely estimate a residual covariance.






TipWhy local independence is the LCA analogue of common-factor models



A CFA models indicator covariances as arising from latent factors plus residuals. LCA models indicator covariances as arising entirely from class membership. Local independence is the corresponding assumption — if violated, the model is mis-specified the same way a CFA is mis-specified by an unmodelled cross-loading.

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
Estimation
LCA and LPA parameters are estimated via Maximum Likelihood, almost always using the EM algorithm (Collins & Lanza, 2010, Ch 4). Two specifically LCA-ish estimation gotchas:

Label switching
The model is invariant to relabelling of the classes — calling one class “high-risk” and another “low-risk” vs the reverse gives statistically identical likelihoods. EM can converge to either labelling, and across multiple runs the labelling can change between solutions. This is harmless for the likelihood but it makes parameter comparisons across runs / draws messy. Report the substantive labels you’ve assigned to classes (based on their item-response probabilities), not the software-default numbering.


Multiple starting values are mandatory
The LCA likelihood is multi-modal — local maxima are common. Always run from many (e.g. 100–1000) random starting values and keep the solution with the highest likelihood (Collins & Lanza, 2010, §4.4–4.5). If your “best” solution is not replicated across multiple starts, the model is empirically unidentified at that class size — try fewer classes or simplify the model.

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
How many classes?
This is the central question in LCA, and there is no single oracle (Collins & Lanza, 2010, §4.6). The standard workflow is to fit a series of solutions (1 class, 2 classes, …, K classes) and compare them on:

Information criteria

BIC (Bayesian information criterion) — usually the default recommended index for LCA; penalises model complexity more heavily than AIC and tends to favour parsimonious solutions.
aBIC (sample-size adjusted BIC; Sclove, 1987) — modified for smaller samples.
AIC — tends to over-extract classes (favours more complex models) and is less commonly used for class enumeration.
CAIC — between BIC and AIC in penalty.

Lower is better for all of these. Plot them as a line across k and look for an elbow (a sharp slowing of improvement) — the k at the elbow is the parsimonious choice.


Likelihood-ratio tests for k vs (k − 1) classes
The standard χ² difference test does not apply to comparing mixture models with different numbers of classes (regularity conditions are violated). Use instead:

Lo-Mendell-Rubin adjusted LRT (LMR) — a parametric correction. Significant p means k classes fit better than k − 1.
Bootstrap Likelihood Ratio Test (BLRT) — non-parametric; widely considered more reliable. Same interpretation.

Both are available in Mplus.


G² and absolute fit (for LCA only)
The likelihood-ratio G² statistic compares observed and expected response-pattern frequencies against a χ² distribution (Collins & Lanza, 2010, §4.3.2). Use with caution: with even modestly many items, the response-pattern contingency table becomes sparse (N / W < 5), and the χ² approximation breaks down. Sparseness is the rule, not the exception — most LCA solutions can’t be tested for absolute fit this way.

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
section: How well does each class hang together?
text:
How well does each class hang together?
Even after picking k, you should check that the classes are substantively interpretable and well-separated.

Homogeneity
A class is homogeneous if its item-response probabilities are close to 0 or close to 1 — i.e. members of the class share a clear response profile (Collins & Lanza, 2010, §3.3). Probabilities clustered around .5 mean the class doesn’t have a distinct profile on that item.


Latent class separation
Separation is the extent to which the classes have distinguishable item-response patterns. High homogeneity within classes plus large differences in those probabilities between classes = good separation. You can have high homogeneity but poor separation (two classes are both homogeneous but look similar) — in which case the second class isn’t really informative.


Posterior classification quality
Each respondent has an estimated posterior probability of belonging to each class. A model with good separation will assign respondents to classes with posteriors near 1; a model with poor separation will produce posteriors near 1/k (essentially guessing).








Diagnostic
Definition
Threshold for “good”




Mean posterior probability per class
Average posterior of being in class c, among respondents assigned to c
≥ .80 (rule of thumb)


Odds of correct classification (OCC)
The class-specific assignment accuracy normalised by chance (Nagin, 2005)
≥ 5 per class (Nagin, 2005, pp. 88–89)


Entropy (Ramaswamy et al., 1993)
A single-number summary of overall classification certainty; 0 ≤ E ≤ 1
Higher = better separation









WarningDon’t use entropy for class enumeration



Entropy is useful for describing the separation quality of a chosen model — but it tends to decrease as the number of classes increases for purely artefactual reasons. Picking the k with highest entropy will systematically under-extract classes. Use BIC and LRT-based tests for class enumeration, and entropy for post-hoc interpretation only (Collins & Lanza, 2010, §3.5).

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
Beyond a single cross-section

Multiple-group LCA
Asks whether the same latent class structure holds across groups (e.g. men vs women, cohorts), and whether classes are equally prevalent across them. The analogue of measurement invariance testing in CFA: fit a configural model and progressively constrain item-response probabilities to equality (Collins & Lanza, 2010, Ch 5). Partial measurement invariance is also possible (§5.10).


LCA with covariates
Once classes are identified, you may want to predict class membership from individual-level covariates (sex, age, intervention condition) — this is a multinomial logistic regression of class membership on the covariates. Two ways to do this: (a) the one-step approach, where covariates enter the LCA model directly, and (b) the three-step approach (Vermunt, 2010), where you fit the LCA, assign cases to their most likely class, and regress class on covariates with a correction for assignment error. The three-step approach is now the standard recommendation (Collins & Lanza, 2010, Ch 6).


Latent Transition Analysis (LTA)
The longitudinal extension. Each respondent is in some class at each measurement occasion, and the model estimates transition probabilities between classes across occasions (Collins & Lanza, 2010, Ch 7–8). Useful for studying stage-sequential development, recovery patterns, intervention effects on group membership.

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP

JASP does not currently have a dedicated LCA / LPA module in its standard distribution. For these analyses, the field’s standard software is:

Mplus (Muthén & Muthén) — the de facto reference for mixture modelling. Long history; comprehensive feature set (multiple-group, covariates, three-step, LTA, growth mixture models). Commercial.
R poLCA (Linzer & Lewis, 2011) — open-source LCA for categorical indicators.
R tidyLPA (Rosenberg et al.) — accessible LPA wrapper around mclust; integrates with the tidyverse.
R mclust (Scrucca et al., 2016) — general-purpose Gaussian mixture modelling, including LPA.
R depmixS4 — LCA + hidden Markov models for transitions over time.
Latent GOLD — another commercial alternative with a strong GUI.

If you’re new to mixture modelling and have continuous indicators, tidyLPA is the gentlest entry point. For categorical LCA in R, poLCA is the standard.

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
Reporting






TipReporting an LCA / LPA study



At minimum:

Conceptual rationale: why a person-centred / mixture approach for this question, not a variable-centred one.
Indicators: the set of items / scales used as class indicators, with descriptive statistics and (for LCA) frequency tables.
Model selection: a table of fit indices (BIC, aBIC, AIC, LMR, BLRT, entropy) for k = 1, 2, …, K classes; explicit justification for the chosen k combining statistical fit and interpretability.
Estimation details: software + version, number of random starting values used, whether the best log-likelihood was replicated.
Class characterisation: the item-response probabilities (LCA) or within-class means and SDs (LPA), in a table or profile plot; a substantive label per class.
Class quality: mean posterior probability per class, OCC values, and overall entropy.
Substantive interpretation: what each class represents, with appropriate hedging if separation is modest.
For multi-group / covariate / longitudinal extensions: details of which approach was used (one-step vs three-step; configural vs invariant; etc.).

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
Further reading

Collins, L. M., & Lanza, S. T. (2010). Latent class and latent transition analysis: With applications in the social, behavioral, and health sciences. Wiley. — The standard reference: model + notation (Ch 2), measurement quality (Ch 3), estimation and model selection (Ch 4), multi-group LCA (Ch 5), covariates (Ch 6), latent transition models (Ch 7–8).
Masyn, K. E. (2013). Latent class analysis and finite mixture modeling. In T. D. Little (Ed.), The Oxford handbook of quantitative methods (Vol. 2). Oxford University Press. — Compact chapter-length treatment with up-to-date model-selection recommendations.
Hagenaars, J. A., & McCutcheon, A. L. (Eds.). (2002). Applied latent class analysis. Cambridge University Press. — Edited handbook with substantive applications.
Nylund-Gibson, K., & Choi, A. Y. (2018). Ten frequently asked questions about latent class analysis. Translational Issues in Psychological Science, 4(4), 440–461. — Excellent FAQ-style introduction.
Berlin, K. S., Williams, N. A., & Parra, G. R. (2014). An introduction to latent variable mixture modeling (part 1): Overview and cross-sectional latent class and latent profile analyses. Journal of Pediatric Psychology, 39(2), 174–187. — Accessible applied tutorial.
Vermunt, J. K. (2010). Latent class modeling with covariates: Two improved three-step approaches. Political Analysis, 18(4), 450–469. — The three-step covariate approach.
Nagin, D. S. (2005). Group-based modeling of development. Harvard University Press. — Source of the OCC diagnostic.

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
Advanced Topics
Measurement invariance (MI) testing asks whether the measurement model of a questionnaire or test behaves the same way across groups — different cultures, sexes, age cohorts, clinical groups, or repeated time points. It is a prerequisite for almost any meaningful between-group comparison of a latent construct: a difference in observed mean scores is only interpretable as a difference on the underlying construct if the items measure that construct equivalently in both groups.
This page builds on the Factor Analysis page — invariance testing uses multi-group CFA, so you’ll want to be comfortable with CFA first.

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
What is the question?
Suppose you administer a 5-item depression scale to a Dutch sample and a UK sample, and want to compare mean depression scores. Before you can interpret a 0.4-point difference as “the Dutch are more depressed”, you need to rule out competing explanations such as:

Do the five items load on the same factor in both samples? (Maybe one item taps something other than depression in the Dutch version)
Do they load to the same degree? (Maybe one item is a stronger indicator of depression in one group than in the other)
Are there systematic offsets in how each group responds to specific items, independent of their true level on the construct? (Maybe a translation makes one item read more strongly in one language)

MI testing addresses each of these as a nested hierarchy of CFA models. If a given level holds, you’ve ruled out one class of confound and can move on to the next.

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
The hierarchy of invariance
Following Meredith (1993) and Vandenberg & Lance (2000), MI is tested as a step-up sequence of increasingly restrictive multi-group CFA models. Brown (2015, Ch 7) and others recommend the descriptive pedagogical names alongside the technical labels:








Level
What is constrained equal across groups
What it allows you to compare




Configural (“equal form”)
Same factor structure (which items load on which factors) — but loadings, intercepts, and residuals all freely estimated
The qualitative pattern of the construct


Metric (“equal factor loadings”, weak factorial)
Configural + factor loadings (λ)
Relationships involving the factor (correlations, regressions, interactions with other variables)


Scalar (“equal intercepts”, strong factorial)
Metric + indicator intercepts (τ)
Latent means — the headline thing you usually want


Strict (“equal residual variances”, strict factorial)
Scalar + indicator residual variances (θ)
Sometimes required for very strict comparisons of observed score reliability across groups; rarely tested in practice









TipWhy the hierarchy is ordered this way



Each step rests on the previous one. You can only compare relationships between factors if loadings are equal (metric); you can only compare latent means if intercepts are also equal (scalar). If scalar invariance fails for some items, you can sometimes proceed with partial invariance (see below).

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
Recommended sequence (Brown, 2015, p. 243)

Fit the CFA model separately in each group — make sure the basic measurement model fits in each before trying to test invariance.
Configural model — fit the multi-group CFA with the same factor structure but no equality constraints. This is the baseline.
Metric model — constrain factor loadings equal across groups. Compare to configural via a nested χ² test (or ΔCFI / ΔRMSEA — see below).
Scalar model — additionally constrain intercepts equal. Compare to metric.
Strict model (optional) — additionally constrain residual variances equal. Compare to scalar.

If scalar invariance holds, you may then proceed to substantive group comparisons:

Test equality of factor variances across groups (do the groups differ in how much they vary on the construct?).
Test equality of factor covariances (if >1 factor) (do the inter-factor relationships differ?).
Test equality of latent means (this is the question you usually want to answer).

Steps 2–5 are tests of measurement invariance (do the items work the same?). Steps 6–8 are tests of population heterogeneity (do the groups differ on the construct itself?).

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
How to judge each step
Brown (2015, §“Reliance on χ²”) notes that the literature uses two kinds of criteria, and most applied papers report both:

Nested χ² difference test
The traditional method. The constrained model has more degrees of freedom; if adding the constraints produces a significant increase in χ² relative to the less-constrained model, the constraint is rejected and you have noninvariance.
Limitation: χ² is extremely sensitive to sample size. With large N, even trivial group differences will produce a significant Δχ², and you’ll reject invariance for substantively meaningless reasons.


Practical fit-index thresholds
Cheung & Rensvold (2002) and Meade, Johnson & Braddy (2008) proposed cut-offs based on Monte Carlo simulations of how much CFI should drop under “true” noninvariance. Common practical rules of thumb:







Criterion
Suggested cutoff for “invariance holds”




ΔCFI
≤ .01 (Cheung & Rensvold, 2002) — or ≤ .002 (Meade et al., 2008, more conservative)


ΔRMSEA
≤ .015 (Chen, 2007)


ΔSRMR
≤ .030 (Chen, 2007) for metric; ≤ .015 for scalar



Report both Δχ² and practical-fit changes. When they disagree (Δχ² significant but ΔCFI < .01), the standard interpretation is that the χ² is being driven by sample size and the substantive impact is small — but document this transparently.

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
Partial invariance
If a step fails for all items, the level of invariance is rejected and you can’t proceed. But if it fails because of one or two specific items, you can sometimes still make group comparisons by freeing the constraint just on those items — this is partial invariance (Byrne, Shavelson & Muthén, 1989).
How to find non-invariant parameters: inspect modification indices for the constrained parameters. A large MI on an intercept (for example) means freeing that constraint would meaningfully improve fit. The standard workflow:

Identify the parameter with the largest MI.
Free it. Re-test. If fit is now acceptable, that parameter is the source of the noninvariance.
Repeat if necessary, but be conservative — freeing too many parameters undermines the meaning of the comparison.







WarningHow much partial invariance is too much?



A common rule of thumb: at least two indicators per factor must remain invariant for latent-mean comparisons to be defensible. Beyond that, the literature is mixed — see Putnick & Bornstein (2016) for current reporting conventions.

title: Measurement Invariance
section: MIMIC modeling: a more parsimonious alternative
text:
MIMIC modeling: a more parsimonious alternative
When you have many groups and a complex factor model, full multi-group CFA can become unwieldy. MIMIC (Multiple Indicators Multiple Causes) is an alternative that codes group membership as a covariate (dummy variable) in a single-group CFA:

A significant direct effect of the dummy code on the latent factor indicates a group difference on the latent mean (population heterogeneity).
A significant direct effect of the dummy code on an indicator indicates measurement noninvariance on that intercept (differential item functioning).

MIMIC is more parsimonious and works well with many groups or small samples, but it can only test two of the four sources of invariance (intercepts and factor means — not loadings or residuals). For full invariance testing, multi-group CFA remains the gold standard (Brown, 2015, §“CFA in Multiple Groups”).

title: Measurement Invariance
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
JASP’s SEM module wraps lavaan and supports multi-group CFA with parameter constraints expressed directly in lavaan syntax. The workflow below follows the JASP-blog tutorial by van Kesteren & Koch (2022), which uses the Holzinger & Swineford (1939) three-factor mental-abilities dataset (nine items grouped into Visual, Textual, and Speed factors) compared across two schools (Pasteur vs Grant-White).

1. Specify the base CFA model
Open the SEM analysis. In the Multigroup SEM tab at the bottom of the input panel, drag the grouping variable (e.g. school) into the Group box. In the Model Options tab, tick Include mean structure so that intercepts will be estimated (required for scalar invariance later). Then enter the configural model syntax in the top input box:
visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3
textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6
speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9







The base CFA: three factors (visual, textual, speed) each measured by three items.


Press Ctrl+Enter to fit the model. JASP estimates this configural model separately in each group (all parameters free).







The SEM model panel after entering the configural syntax for Model 1.




2. Add the metric model with constrained loadings
Click the green + next to “Model 1” to add a Model 2. To constrain factor loadings equal across groups, label each loading with a name*item prefix — identical labels are interpreted by lavaan as equality constraints across groups:
visual  =~ v1*x1 + v2*x2 + v3*x3
textual =~ t1*x4 + t2*x5 + t3*x6
speed   =~ s1*x7 + s2*x8 + s3*x9
Press Ctrl+Enter again. JASP now fits Model 2 alongside Model 1 and reports a model-comparison table with χ², df, ΔCFI, AIC/BIC, and the χ² difference test:







Model 1 (configural) vs Model 2 (metric / loadings constrained). The difference test is non-significant (Δχ²(6) = 8.19, p = .224), so metric invariance is supported.




3. Add the scalar model with constrained intercepts
Click + again to add a Model 3. In addition to the labelled loadings, label the indicator intercepts explicitly (the ~ i*1 syntax constrains the intercept of each indicator equal across groups):
visual  =~ v1*x1 + v2*x2 + v3*x3
textual =~ t1*x4 + t2*x5 + t3*x6
speed   =~ s1*x7 + s2*x8 + s3*x9
x1 ~ i1*1
x2 ~ i2*1
x3 ~ i3*1
x4 ~ i4*1
x5 ~ i5*1
x6 ~ i6*1
x7 ~ i7*1
x8 ~ i8*1
x9 ~ i9*1
The model-comparison table now compares all three nested models in one place:







All three nested models. In this example metric invariance held (Δχ² p = .224 between Models 1 and 2) but scalar invariance was rejected (Δχ²(9) = 40.06, p < .001 between Models 2 and 3) — meaning the items’ baseline levels differ between schools and latent-mean comparisons are not defensible without further partial-invariance work.








TipWhy type the constraints by hand?



JASP exposes “Constrain loadings / intercepts / residual variances equal across groups” as click-boxes in the point-and-click interface — but using them, you cannot produce the nested model-comparison table with χ² difference tests shown above. The labelled-syntax workflow gives you the formal invariance test in one click.


For automated MI testing across the configural → metric → scalar → strict sequence outside JASP, the standard R tool is semTools::measEq.syntax() (or, for quick exploration, semTools::measurementInvariance() in older versions). Results from R can be reported alongside the JASP fit output.
→ Full tutorial: van Kesteren, E.-J., & Koch, M. (2022). Measurement invariance testing using the SEM module in JASP — JASP blog. Walks through the worked example above end to end.

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
Reporting






TipReporting MI testing



Following Putnick & Bornstein (2016), state:

The grouping variable and groups, with sample sizes per group.
The base CFA model that was fit in each group (which items, which factors), with fit indices per group.
A fit table with one row per nested model (configural, metric, scalar, [strict]). Columns: χ², df, p, CFI, RMSEA, SRMR, Δχ²/Δdf relative to the previous step, p of Δχ², ΔCFI, ΔRMSEA.
Decision at each step (invariant or not), and any parameters freed for partial invariance.
Substantive interpretation of any latent-mean differences at the end of the sequence.

Example sentence: “Configural invariance held (CFI = .968, RMSEA = .041). Imposing equal loadings did not significantly worsen fit (Δχ²(4) = 5.21, p = .27, ΔCFI = .001), supporting metric invariance. Imposing equal intercepts produced a significant Δχ² (Δχ²(4) = 22.4, p < .001) but a small change in CFI (ΔCFI = .005). One item (item 3) showed a large modification index; freeing its intercept restored fit and supported partial scalar invariance.”

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
Further reading

Brown, T. A. (2015). Confirmatory factor analysis for applied research (2nd ed.). Guilford Press. — Ch 7 covers multi-group CFA, MI testing, and partial invariance in depth.
Kline, R. B. (2023). Principles and practice of structural equation modeling (5th ed.). Guilford Press. — Ch 12 covers multi-group SEM more broadly (path-model and full-SEM cases, not just CFA).
Putnick, D. L., & Bornstein, M. H. (2016). Measurement invariance conventions and reporting: The state of the art and future directions for psychological research. Developmental Review, 41, 71–90. — Current reporting standards.
Vandenberg, R. J., & Lance, C. E. (2000). A review and synthesis of the measurement invariance literature. Organizational Research Methods, 3(1), 4–70. — The classic taxonomy paper.
Meredith, W. (1993). Measurement invariance, factor analysis and factorial invariance. Psychometrika, 58(4), 525–543. — Source of the original “factorial invariance” terminology (weak / strong / strict).
Chen, F. F. (2007). Sensitivity of goodness of fit indexes to lack of measurement invariance. Structural Equation Modeling, 14(3), 464–504. — Source of the ΔRMSEA / ΔSRMR thresholds.
Cheung, G. W., & Rensvold, R. B. (2002). Evaluating goodness-of-fit indexes for testing measurement invariance. Structural Equation Modeling, 9(2), 233–255. — Source of the ΔCFI ≤ .01 threshold.
Meade, A. W., Johnson, E. C., & Braddy, P. W. (2008). Power and sensitivity of alternative fit indices in tests of measurement invariance. Journal of Applied Psychology, 93(3), 568–592. — More conservative thresholds (ΔCFI ≤ .002) from a larger simulation.
Byrne, B. M., Shavelson, R. J., & Muthén, B. (1989). Testing for the equivalence of factor covariance and mean structures: The issue of partial measurement invariance. Psychological Bulletin, 105(3), 456–466. — The foundational partial-invariance paper.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Advanced Topics
Use logistic regression when your outcome variable is categorical — most commonly binary (yes/no, clinical/control, recovered/not recovered). Linear regression is inappropriate for binary outcomes because it can produce predicted probabilities below 0 or above 1, and it violates homoscedasticity.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
What is it?
Binary logistic regression models the log-odds (logit) of the outcome as a linear function of the predictors:
\[\log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = b_0 + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots\]
The model predicts the probability that a case belongs to the “outcome = 1” category. Predictors can be continuous, binary, or categorical (with dummy coding).

Odds ratios
The exponentiated coefficients (e^b) are odds ratios (OR):







OR
Interpretation




> 1
A one-unit increase in X is associated with increased odds of the outcome


< 1
A one-unit increase in X is associated with decreased odds of the outcome


= 1
No association









NoteOdds ≠ probability



An OR of 2 does not mean the outcome is “twice as likely” — it means the odds are doubled. Probabilities and odds are related but not the same. Always report predicted probabilities or absolute risk when communicating results to a non-statistical audience.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
When to use logistic regression







Situation
Appropriate method




Binary outcome (2 categories)
Binary logistic regression


Nominal outcome (3+ unordered categories)
Multinomial logistic regression


Ordinal outcome (3+ ordered categories)
Ordinal logistic regression



This page focuses on binary logistic regression — the most common case in psychology research.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Assumptions


Binary outcome: the DV must have exactly two categories
Independence of observations: each participant contributes one row
No multicollinearity: predictors should not be highly correlated — check VIF as in regular regression
Sufficient cell sizes: especially important when predictors are categorical; very small cells can cause convergence problems
No complete separation: if a predictor perfectly predicts the outcome, the model cannot estimate coefficients
Linearity of continuous predictors with the log-odds: check using Box-Tidwell test or visual inspection (available as an option in JASP)


Note: logistic regression does not require normality of residuals or homoscedasticity — these are linear regression assumptions that do not apply here.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Model fit
Unlike linear regression, logistic regression uses maximum likelihood estimation. Model fit statistics:







Statistic
What it tells you




−2 Log Likelihood (−2LL)
Overall fit — smaller is better


Nagelkerke R²
Pseudo-R²: approximate proportion of variance explained — treat as an effect size, not a literal R²


Hosmer-Lemeshow test
Goodness of fit — a non-significant result indicates adequate fit


Classification table
Percentage of cases correctly classified by the model



Model comparison: test whether adding predictors improves fit using the likelihood ratio test (χ² difference in −2LL with corresponding df).

title: Logistic Regression
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Go to Regression → Logistic Regression.






NoteJASP Video Library — Logistic Regression



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#logistic-regression:

▶ Logistic Regression — setup, odds ratios, classification table, model fit

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Interpreting the output
Omnibus test: tests whether the model with predictors fits better than an intercept-only model. A significant χ² (p < .05) indicates the model explains a significant amount of variance.
Coefficients table: for each predictor, report: - b: the log-odds coefficient (hard to interpret directly) - OR (= e^b): the odds ratio — more interpretable - 95% CI for the OR: does it include 1.0? If not, the predictor is significant - p-value






TipAPA reporting example



Logistic regression was used to predict survival from age and treatment group. The model was significant, χ²(2) = 14.3, p < .001, Nagelkerke R² = .23. Treatment group was a significant predictor, b = 1.42, OR = 4.14, 95% CI [1.87, 9.17], p = .001.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Multinomial logistic regression
When the outcome has three or more unordered categories, use multinomial logistic regression. This fits separate binary logistic regressions comparing each category to a reference category, but estimates them jointly.
In JASP, multinomial logistic regression lives under the Generalized Linear Model interface. Go to Regression → Generalized Linear Model, set Family to Other and Model to Multinomial logistic regression, then drag the categorical outcome into Dependent Variable and your predictors into Continuous Predictors / Categorical Predictors.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Further reading

DSUJ Chapter 17 — Logistic regression — binary and multinomial logistic regression with JASP walkthroughs

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Try it yourself
Each exercise below ends with a collapsible solution. Try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

Exercise 1 (binary logistic regression — risk factors) — Which factors predict adolescent suicide risk?
Adolescents at risk of self-harm were measured on demographics, family environment, and music preferences (heavy metal among others). Using leni_17.jasp, fit a binary logistic regression with risk status as the outcome. Report each predictor’s odds ratio with its 95% CI and the Nagelkerke R².
